Sodium-ion batteries: present and future
Jang-Yeon Hwang,\(^{a}\) Seung-Taek Myung\(^{b}\) and Yang-Kook Sun\(^{\star a}\)

Energy production and storage technologies have attracted a great deal of attention for day-to-day applications. In recent decades, advances in lithium-ion battery (LIB) technology have improved living conditions around the globe. LIBs are used in most mobile electronic devices as well as in zero-emission electronic vehicles. However, there are increasing concerns regarding load leveling of renewable energy sources and the smart grid as well as the sustainability of lithium sources due to their limited availability and consequent expected price increase. Therefore, whether LIBs alone can satisfy the rising demand for small- and/or mid-to-large-format energy storage applications remains unclear. To mitigate these issues, recent research has focused on alternative energy storage systems. Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are considered as the best candidate power sources because sodium is widely available and exhibits similar chemistry to that of LIBs; therefore, SIBs are promising next-generation alternatives. Recently, sodiated layer transition metal oxides, phosphates and organic compounds have been introduced as cathode materials for SIBs. Simultaneously, recent developments have been facilitated by the use of select carbonaceous materials, transition metal oxides (or sulfides), and intermetallic and organic compounds as anodes for SIBs. Apart from electrode materials, suitable electrolytes, additives, and binders are equally important for the development of practical SIBs. Despite developments in electrode materials and other components, there remain several challenges, including cell design and electrode balancing, in the application of sodium ion cells. In this article, we summarize and discuss current research on materials and propose future directions for SIBs. This will provide important insights into scientific and practical issues in the development of SIBs.

1. Introduction

Fossil fuels are the most widely used energy resource worldwide. Risks related to resource depletion, environmental pollution, and political unrest with regard to fossil fuel production have led to the rapid emergence of a variety of intermittent renewable and cleaner energy sources such as wind, solar and wave.

In order to integrate these renewable energies into the electrical grid, a large-scale energy storage system (ESS) is vital to peak shift operation. Among various energy storage technologies, using an electrochemical secondary battery is a promising method for large-scale storage of electricity due to its flexibility, high energy conversion efficiency, and simple maintenance. LIBs, which have become common power sources in the portable electronic market since their first commercialization by Sony in the early 1990s, are the primary candidates for ESSs. The introduction of LIBs into the automotive market as the battery of choice for powering hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs) and electric vehicles (EVs) could reduce dependence on fossil fuels. Lithium, the primary ingredient in LIBs, is non-uniformly distributed within the Earth's crust. As a result, the Andean states have been dubbed the 'new Middle-East'. However, the increasing demand for lithium associated with these new and large-scale applications is expected to skyrocket the price of lithium, affecting reserves as well, as it is not a naturally abundant element. Based on the calculations, overall global Li consumption in 2008 was nearly 21280 tons; hence, present mineable resources could be sustained for approximately 65 years at most at an average growth rate of \(5\%\) per year,[2,5] making the implementation of the above-mentioned applications difficult and very costly.

Sodium, the fourth most abundant element on earth, has a seemingly unlimited distribution. Supplies of sodium-containing precursors are vast, with 23 billion tons of soda ash located in the United States alone. The abundance of resources and the much lower cost of trona (about \(135 - 165\) per ton), from which sodium carbonate is produced, compared to lithium carbonate (about \)5000$ per ton in 2010), provide a compelling rationale for the development of SIBs to be used as alternatives to LIBs.[7,8] Because an alternative to lithium is needed to realize large-scale applications, SIBs have attracted considerable research attention in recent years. SIBs were initially studied when the development of LIBs began in the 1970s and 1980s, but due to rapid advances in the development and success of commercial applications of LIBs, SIBs were largely abandoned.[9-15] Moreover, during those years, the overall quality of materials, electrolytes and glove

boxes was insufficient for handling sodium, making it difficult to observe electrode performance. In the 1980s, prior to the commercialization of LIBS, a few US and Japanese companies developed SIBs in full cell configurations where a sodium-lead alloy composite and a P2-type \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) were used as the anode and cathode, respectively. Despite the remarkable cyclability over 300 cycles, the average discharge voltages were lower than \(3.0\mathrm{V}\), which did not attract much attention against carbon// \(\mathrm{LiCoO}_2\) cells exhibiting an average discharge voltage of \(3.7\mathrm{V}\). The battery components and the electrical storage mechanism of SIBs and LIBs are basically the same except for their ion carriers. In terms of cathode materials, the intercalation chemistry of sodium is very similar to that of lithium, making it possible to use similar compounds for both systems. However, there are some obvious differences between these systems. \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions \((1.02\AA)\) are larger compared to \(\mathrm{Li}^+\) ions \((0.76\AA)\), which affects the phase stability, transport properties, and interphase formation. Sodium is also heavier than lithium \((23\mathrm{g mol}^{-1}\) compared to \(6.9\mathrm{g mol}^{-1}\)) and has a higher standard electrode potential \((-2.71\mathrm{V}\) vs. SHE as compared to \(-3.02\mathrm{V}\) vs. SHE for lithium); thus, SIBs will always fall short in terms of energy density. However, the weight of cyclable Li or Na is a small fraction of the mass of the components, and the capacity is determined primarily by the characteristics of the host structures that serve as electrodes. Hence, in principle, there should be no energy density consequences of the transition from LIBs to SIBs. In addition, aluminum undergoes alloy reaction with lithium below \(0.1\mathrm{V}\) vs. \(\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Li}^+\), which indicates that aluminum is available as a current collector for anodes in sodium cells. Therefore, aluminum is a cost-effective alternative to copper as an anode current collector for SIBs.

Various cathode materials for SIBs have been reported; for instance, layer and tunnel type transition metal oxides, transition metal sulfides and fluorides, oxyanionic compounds, Prussian blue analogues and polymers. However, the search for an anode with appropriate Na voltage storage, a large reversible capacity, and high structural stability remains an obstacle to development of SIBs. Graphite, which is a common anode material in LIBs, has a moderate Li storage capacity \((\sim 350\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) at approximately \(0.1\mathrm{V}\) vs. \(\mathrm{Li / Li^{+}}\). Recent studies have demonstrated that graphite does not properly intercalate sodium ions. Non-graphitic anodes, which consist largely of various carbonaceous materials such as carbon black and pitch-based carbon-fibers, allow insertion of sodium ions. Hard carbons, which are synthesized at high temperatures from carbon-based precursors, have been comprehensively modeled, characterized, and thermally tested in Na cells. These non-graphitic carbonaceous materials are considered to be the "first-generation" anodes of choice for SIB systems. SIBs are not fabricated with sodium metal due to dendrite formation, high reactivity, and an unstable passivation layer in the most organic electrolytes at room temperature. The high reactivity of metallic sodium with organic electrolyte solvents and dendrite formation during Na metal deposition are even more problematic than they are in Li metal anodes. The low melting point of sodium at \(97.7^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) also presents a safety hazard for devices using Na metal electrodes at ambient temperature.

Thus, it is important to use a true Na-ion system, where Na ions are exchanged between cathodes and anodes in a 'rocking-chair' format.

A new type of electrolyte for SIBs is needed, as the use of organic liquid electrolytes raises practicality and safety issues. The most common electrolyte formulations for SIBs are \(\mathrm{NaClO_4}\) or \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\) salts in carbonate ester solvents, particularly propylene carbonate (PC). Metallic sodium anodes corrode continuously in the presence of these organic electrolytes, rather than forming a stable solid electrolyte interface (SEI). According to XPS and TOF-SIMS analyses performed by Komaba et al.,[27] when \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\) is used as the electrolyte salt, the SEI film on hard carbon is predominantly an inorganic salt that contains precipitated species such as NaF on the surface.[7] Palacin and colleagues[28] found that \(\mathrm{NaClO_4}\) and \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\) in an EC:PC solvent mixture represent the best electrolyte for a hard carbon anode. Developing aqueous electrolytes instead of organic electrolytes could be essential to the success of SIBs. Recently, an aqueous rechargeable battery with \(\mathrm{Na_2NiFe(CN)_6}\) and \(\mathrm{NaTi_2(PO_4)}\) as the cathode and anode, respectively, demonstrated a good rate and cycle life with a theoretical energy density of \(42.5\mathrm{Whkg}^{-1}\).[29] Thus, it is possible to achieve higher energy density by selecting the appropriate electrode material. Nevertheless, an aqueous electrolyte system is more complicated than an organic system because of the (1) elimination of residual \(\mathrm{O}_2\) from the electrolyte, (2) maintenance of electrode stability in the aqueous electrolyte, (3) inhibition of \(\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{O}^+\) co-intercalation into the electrode and (4) efficiency of the internal consumption of \(\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\) produced from the cathode and anode sides when overcharged, overdischarged or improperly operated in a closed aqueous battery system. All of these issues are important for practical applications of aqueous battery systems.[30,31]

Scheme 1 and Fig. 1 illustrate a schematic SIB image that can adopt several representative candidate materials such as cathode and anode materials, electrolytes, separators, and binders that are discussed in the present paper. Most studies of SIBs explored the electrochemical performance of new electrodes and materials used with Na metal in half-cells, as this field is still in its early stages, and it is thus difficult to create full cells. In that regard, discussion and justification of SIBs are complicated compared to those of LIBs. In addition, experimental conditions such as the purity of Ar gas, electrolyte quality, and the glove box can influence the performance of SIB cells. Hence, comparison of battery performance between studies can be challenging.[18]
2. Cathode materials

Similar to LIBs, highly reversible cathode materials based on the intercalation reaction, which involves interstitial introduction of a guest species \(\mathrm{(Na^{+}}\) in the present context), are needed for high capacity and good cyclability of SIBs. These electrode materials are mainly categorized into oxides, polyanions such as phosphates, pyrophosphates, fluorosulfates, oxychlorides, and NASICON (Na super ionic conductor) types, and organic compounds, which are mentioned in detail in Sections 2 and 3. These cathode materials exhibit a minimal structural change with intercalation, which ensures a reversible intercalation reaction that affects the cycle life. However, continuous structural evolution is inevitable during \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion intercalation into the host structure interaction because of the large \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion size (coordination number 6: \(1.02\AA\)) relative to that of \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\)

Also, sodiated transition metal materials are highly hygroscopic, even with brief exposure to air,[32] and caution is necessary to avoid hydration of the material, particularly the surface, which results in formation of NaOH that degrades electrode performance due to its insulating properties. Thus, the preparation of sodiated cathode materials and batteries requires meticulous handling and moisture-free conditions.
2.1. Two-dimensional layer transition metal oxides

The above-mentioned prototype compounds are composed of two- and three-dimensional crystal structures. Early investigation of two-dimensional layer oxides was performed by Delmas and Hagenmuller in the early 1980s.[13,14,33] They defined the crystal structure of layered compounds depending on the stacking sequence of alkali ions between layers. Sodiated transition metal oxides, \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{MO}_2\) (M: transition metal), were representatively classified into two main groups, O3 type and P2 type (Fig. 2), by Delmas et al.[33] Those crystal structures comprise sheets of edge-sharing \(\mathrm{MO}_6\) octahedral layers sandwiched between Na ion layers into which ionic species are inserted in an octahedral (O) or a prismatic (P) environment. The number (O2, O3, P2, and P3) indicates the packing number of Na ion octahedral or prismatic layers within each unit cell. In addition, the prime symbol ('') indicates monoclinic distortion, such that \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\) and \(\mathrm{P}^{\prime}3\) represent monoclinic distortion of the O3 and P3 phases, respectively.

The O3 type is stable when the \(x\) value is high in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{MO}_2\) (\(x\) is close to 0), in which the average oxidation state of M is close to \(3+\). Electrochemical de-/sodiation of the O3 structure

progresses with reversible structural transformation of \(\mathrm{O}3\leftrightarrow \mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\leftrightarrow \mathrm{P}3\leftrightarrow \mathrm{P}^{\prime}3.\) \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions energetically favor a prismatic environment when \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are partly extracted from the crystal structure, which creates vacancies. At the same time, this extraction induces strong repulsion of oxygen in the Na layers, and the interlayer distance thus expands. \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) diffusion occurs faster in the \(\mathrm{P}^{\prime}3\) phase due to the greater interlayer distance compared to O3. These transitions are followed by gliding of the \(\mathrm{MO}_2\) slab without breakage of M-O bonds, as proposed by Delmas et al.33

when the Na content is in the range of 0.3–0.7 in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{MO}_2\), in which the average oxidation state of M is above \(3.3+\). The presence of vacancies in the structure causes strong repulsion of oxygen in the Na layers, resulting in expansion of the interlayer distance. This leads to \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions occupying prismatic (P) sites due to the large Na ionic size. \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions occupy two different types of trigonal prismatic sites: \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) (Na1) contacts the two \(\mathrm{MO}_6\) octahedra of the adjacent slabs along its face, whereas \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) (Na2) contacts the six surrounding \(\mathrm{MO}_6\) octahedra along its edges. Adjacent \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) sites are too close together (considering the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ionic radius) to be occupied simultaneously. The P2 phase is maintained in a wide desodiation range up to \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.46}\mathrm{MO}_2\). Further desodiation shifts the phase toward O2 due to gliding (\(\pi/3\) rotation) of the \(\mathrm{MO}_6\) octahedra and contraction of the crystal structure, reducing the interlayer distance. Based on O3 \(\leftrightarrow\) O'3 \(\leftrightarrow\) P'3 the phase transitions for the O3 type and P2 \(\leftrightarrow\) O2 for the P2 type, structural variation from the O3/P3 to P2/O2(OP4) type or vice versa is not possible in Na cells unless the M-O bond breaks. Apart from phase transition, the P3 type can be produced not only via electrochemical desodiation, but also at low temperatures when synthesizing P2 type compounds; P3 and P2 are low and high temperature phases, respectively, indicating that phase transition requires breakage or reformation of M-O bonds via heat treatment.

2.1.1. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{FeO}_2\) and derivatives. The O3 type layers, \(\alpha\)-, \(\beta\)-, and \(\gamma\)-type \(\mathrm{LiFeO}_2\), have advantages including non-toxicity and cost effectiveness related to the abundance of Fe. However, de-intercalation does not occur in Li cells.[34-38] This may be because Fe ion distribution is randomized from the 3b ordered state to a 3b-6c disordered state during charge in \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{LiFeO}_2\), creating a large irreversible capacity.[39,40] Matsumura et al.[41] suggested that \(\mathrm{Li}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Mn}_{1/3}\mathrm{Fe}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\), which contains a mixture of O3, O2 and P2 phases, ionic exchange from \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Mn}_{1/3}\mathrm{Fe}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) showed a reversible charge-discharge capacity of approximately \(70\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in Li cells. Interestingly, Kikkawa et al.[42,43] suggested the possibility of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) deintercalation from \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{NaFeO}_2\), creating \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.9}\mathrm{FeO}_2\), using a chemical oxidizing agent \((\mathrm{Br}_2)\), while Takeda et al.[39] successfully performed electrochemical \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) deintercalation to \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.5}\mathrm{FeO}_2\) with an approximate \(125\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) capacity whereas lithium metal was used as the anode (Fig. 3a), which accompanied phase transformation into the monoclinic P3 phase on charge. Further investigation using Mössbauer spectroscopy indicated that the \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+/4+}\) redox reaction was responsible for capacity delivery. Approximately \(70\%\) of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions could be extracted when charging to \(4.5\mathrm{V}\), but almost no capacity was delivered on discharge, which was possibly disturbed by iron ion migration. When \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions are extracted from the host structure, vacancies are created at tetrahedral sites contacting \(\mathrm{FeO}_6\) octahedral faces. As a result, trivalent iron ions are energetically stabilized at tetrahedral sites and iron ions migrate to the shared face, similar to the phenomena observed in \(\mathrm{LiCo}_{1-x}\mathrm{Fe}_x\mathrm{O}_2\).[44] Although the capacity was limited and the material suffered from the irreversible structural change, the electrochemically desodiated \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.58}\mathrm{FeO}_2\) exothermically decomposed above \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) with much lower heat generation compared with that

\((102\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\). Wang et al. [48] stabilized the crystal structure of \(\mathrm{NaFeO_2}\) by forming a solid solution with \(\mathrm{NaNiO_2}\), \(\mathrm{NaFe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Ni}_x\mathrm{O}_2\) \((0\leq x\leq 1)\). The dilution of the Fe concentration, \(\mathrm{NaFe_{0.3}Ni_{0.7}O_2}\), effectively increased capacity and retention ( \(135~\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) and \(74\%\) retention after 30 cycles) via \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3 + / 4 + }\) and \(\mathrm{Ni}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox couples (Fig. 3b). In addition, they also recorded the \(^{57}\mathrm{Fe}\) Mössbauer spectra to determine the valence state and investigate the mechanism of the electrochemical reaction of Fe in O3- \(\mathrm{NaFe}_{1 - y}\mathrm{Ni}_y\mathrm{O}_2\) \((y = 0,0.5,\) and 0.7). Recent report by Nanba et al. also well supported the electrochemical reaction mechanism of \(\mathrm{NaFe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Ni}_x\mathrm{O}_2\) \((0\leq x\leq 1)\) electrodes.[49]

Extension of the layer structure toward a three component system, \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Fe}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{1/3}] \mathrm{O}_2\), was suggested by Kim et al. For synthesis, they used oxalate coprecipitation, \([\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Fe}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{1/3}] \mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) due to the difficulty of forming hydroxides when Fe is involved. \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Fe}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{1/3}] \mathrm{O}_2\) could deliver a discharge capacity of \(120 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\) in the voltage range of \(2-4 \mathrm{~V}\) for a half cell. They also tested the cyclability after adopting a hard carbon anode (\(100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\) after 150 cycles). Oh et al. also synthesized spherical \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.25}\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.25}] \mathrm{O}_2\) using \([\mathrm{Ni}_{0.25}\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.25}](\mathrm{OH})_2\). They found that the electronic states of Ni, Fe, and Mn were \(2+\), \(3+\), and \(4+\), respectively, of which \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+ / 4+}\) and \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+ / 4+}\) were responsible for electrochemical activity in the range of \(2.1-3.9 \mathrm{~V}\) via the X-ray absorption nearest edge spectral (XANES) analysis technique. Mn remained electrochemically inactive, but preserved the crystal structure during the electrochemical reaction. A \(\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{O}_4 / \mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.25}\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Co}_{0.25}] \mathrm{O}_2\) full cell with a conversion anode and an insertion cathode delivered a capacity of approximately \(130 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~h} (\mathrm{g}-\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.25}\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.25}] \mathrm{O}_2)^{-1}\) with approximately \(76.1\%\) retention at the 150th cycle (Fig. 3c).

Despite the good reversibility of \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.25}\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.25}]\mathrm{O}_2\), the discharge capacity limit of \(140\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in O3 type layer materials needs to be overcome. The voltage limitation to \(3.9\mathrm{V}\) prevents iron migration during charge. Oh et al. suggested another approach to raise the upper voltage cutoff to \(4.4\mathrm{V}\), in order to offer more capacity in O3 type compounds using the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+ / 4+}\) redox, but reducing the concentration of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) in \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Li}_{0.05}(\mathrm{Ni}_{0.25}\mathrm{Fe}_{0.25}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5})_{0.95}]\mathrm{O}_2\). As anticipated the compound was active based on the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+ / 4+}\), \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+ / 4+}\), and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+ / 4+}\) redox reactions, resulting in a large capacity of approximately \(180\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at a 0.1C-rate in the voltage range of \(1.7-4.4\mathrm{V}\). Additionally, the presence of a stronger Li-O bond relative to that of Ni-O and Mn-O in the transition metal layer was responsible for stabilization of the crystal structure, enabling better capacity retention during cycling.

2.1.2. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 - y}\mathrm{Mn}_y]\mathrm{O}_2\) (\(x\leq 0.3\)) and derivatives. As mentioned in Section 2.1.1, O3 type compounds deliver limited reversible capacities of less than \(160\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\), of which the O3 phase follows a reversible structural transformation (O3 \(\leftrightarrow\) O'3 \(\leftrightarrow\) P3 \(\leftrightarrow\) P'3). Further desodiation results in hexagonal P3" from P'3 that shows much greater interslab distances of approximately 7 Å compared to the P'3 phase (5.6 Å).\(^{53}\) This greater distance is not preferred because it induces intercalation of electrolytic molecules formed from oxidative decomposition of electrolytes at high voltage. Hence, delivery of a high capacity of \(180\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) or above is not possible in O3 systems.

\(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{FeO}_2\) is not stable in P2 type layer compounds because of the intrinsic instability of tetravalent Fe in an oxide framework, as described in Section 2.1.1. Yabuuchi and Komaba et al. introduced a \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_x[Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}]O_2}\) layer compound that delivers \(190~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) of reversible capacity (Fig. 4a).<sup>46</sup> Partial substitution of Fe by Mn made it possible to utilize the electrochemical oxidization of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) to \(\mathrm{Fe}^{4+}\) reversibly, in particular in a voltage range of \(3.8 - 4.2\mathrm{V}\). Upon desodiation, the P2 phase was maintained at \(3.8\mathrm{V}\) by the oxidation of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+/4+}\), \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.42}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\), after which the oxidation of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+/4+}\) led to phase transformation from P2 to OP4 at \(4.2\mathrm{V}\) (\(\mathrm{Na}_{0.13}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\)) as shown in Fig. 4b. An in situ XRD study also demonstrated that \(\mathrm{P2 - Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\) undergoes a reversible P2-OP4 phase transition at the end of charge.<sup>54</sup> This achievement is remarkable in terms of capacity in comparison to the O3 type \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\) that delivers less than \(120\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) in early cycles. Despite a high capacity approximating \(190\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) on the first cycle, capacity fading was inevitable in the \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_x[Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}]O_2}\) compound.

Morternard de Boisse et al. raised another issue regarding the structural change during the first discharge from OCV to \(1.5\mathrm{V}\). The P2 phase, \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.62}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\), was transformed into orthorhombic \(\mathrm{P^{\prime}2}\) (space group: Cmcm), \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.97}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\), by cooperative Jahn-Teller distortion at the end of discharge. In a P2 type layer structure, \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) sites are both simultaneously occupied at a ratio close to \(2:1\). This distribution results from the strong sodium-sodium repulsion interaction, which prevails over sodium-transition metal repulsion. Hence, \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) cannot be occupied simultaneously in the P2 phase. All \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ions are located at one site, and the stoichiometry of \(\mathrm{Na[Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}]O_2}\) can be stabilized in the orthorhombic structure. Note that the atomic displacement parameter of Na in the \(\mathrm{P^{\prime}2}\) phase \((0.7(2)\mathring{\mathrm{A}}^{2})\) is much lower than that of the P2 phase \((1.9(3)\mathring{\mathrm{A}}^{2})\), indicating that \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ions are less mobile in the orthorhombic \(\mathrm{P^{\prime}2}\) phase. One interesting finding that contrasts with the results of Yabuuchi and Komaba et al. is that when electrochemical sodiation forms the orthorhombic \(\mathrm{P^{\prime}2}\) phase, the resulting desodiation to \(4.3\mathrm{V}\) leads to the formation of not OP4, but also a new and unindexable "Z" phase \((\mathrm{Na_x[Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}]O_2}, x < 0.25)\) with poor crystallinity (Fig. 4c). Talaie et al. revealed phase "Z", which is a result of migration of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) into tetrahedral sites in the interlayer space, showing a short range order between two adjacent layers. This migration is highly reversible, although it induces polarization of the cell. Addition of Ni instead of Fe was very effective at mitigating migration of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) and thus improved the cycling performance.

Thorne et al. determined the relationship between the Na and Fe content in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Mn}_x]\mathrm{O}_2\) \((0\leq x\leq 0.5)\). The Na content is a decisive factor establishing the phase (O3, O'3, P2, etc.). As clearly seen in Fig. 4d, the length of the first desodiation plateau increased with \(x\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Mn}_x]\mathrm{O}_2\). The length of the first sodiation plateau also increased as \(x\) decreased due to more sodium vacancies in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Mn}_x]\mathrm{O}_2\). The oxidation states in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Mn}_x]\mathrm{O}_2\) are \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\), as suggested by Mossbauer spectroscopy. Therefore, desodiation oxidized \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) to \(\mathrm{Fe}^{4+}\), while the sodiation of \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 - x}\mathrm{Mn}_x]\mathrm{O}_2\) selectively reduced \(\mathrm{Fe}^{4 + / 3 + }\) at high voltages above \(3.5\mathrm{V}\) and reduced

\(\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + / 3 + }\) oxidation below \(3.5\mathrm{V}\). This coincides with the results of Yabuuchi and Komaba. Jung et al.\(^{58}\) explored the effects of Co on \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}[(Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5})_{1 - x}Co_x]O_2}\) (\(x = 0 - 0.2\)). Note that the partial replacement of Fe by Co in \(\mathrm{O3 - Na[Fe_{1 - x}Co_x]O_2}\) dramatically enhanced capacity and performance.\(^{47}\) Similarly, the addition of Co into the transition metal layers of \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}[(Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5})_{1 - x}Co_x]O_2}\) resulted in higher capacity and contributed to stable cycling behavior during cycling, in particular for \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}[(Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5})_{0.8}Co_{0.2}]O_2}\). In contrast to \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_x[Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}]O_2}\), \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}[(Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5})_{0.8}Co_{0.2}]O_2}\) underwent a phase transition toward the O2 phase when desodiated and the O2 phase was transformed into P2 upon sodiation. The P2 to O2 transition was reversibly achieved via the gliding of slabs due to prismatic site instability without \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions. This simple phase transition is related to the addition of Co in the transition metal layers, as a result of structural stabilization. The phase transition from P2 to OP4 is supposed to occur (Fig. 4e); however, the added Co that stabilizes the crystal structure is likely to suppress phase transition towards the OP4 structure because of the suppression of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3 + }\) migration to a tetrahedral or octahedral interspace. Therefore, the simple phase transition from P2 to O2 is responsible for the better capacity retention of Co-doped \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}[(Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5})_{1 - x}Co_x]O_2}\). In comparison with the O3 layer structure, the simple phase transition during the de-/sodiation process of P2 layer compounds is obviously advantageous in preserving the original structure during cycling.

However, an important issue regarding the Na-deficient P2 structure is that such high capacities can be obtained only after the first cycle with a Na metal counter electrode. Compensation of Na in the synthetic state causes formation of an O3 and/or \(\mathrm{O^{\prime}3}\) structure with a further increase in the Na content of \(\mathrm{Na}_x[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 2}]\mathrm{O}_2\) \((x\geq 0.8)\). Therefore, the irreversible capacity of the first cycle is an intrinsic issue with P2 type materials. Singh et al. suggested using a \(\mathrm{NaN_3}\) additive, sacrificial salt, which acts as follows: \(2\mathrm{NaN}_3\rightarrow 3\mathrm{N}_2 + 2\mathrm{Na}^+ +2\mathrm{e}^-\). This oxidative decomposition was effective in circumventing the irreversible capacity loss in \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 2}]\mathrm{O}_2\) in the first cycle. \(\mathrm{NaN_3}\) was used as a source of extra \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions added to the cathode. Hence, \(\mathrm{NaN_3}\)-added P2 \(\mathrm{NaN}_3[\mathrm{Fe}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 2}]\mathrm{O}_2\) could successfully reduce the irreversible first charge capacity from \(58\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) to \(27\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) (Fig. 4f). Taking into account \(\mathbf{N}_2\) release after oxidative decomposition at high potentials, an appropriate amount of \(\mathrm{NaN_3}\) is recommended because decomposition can cause swelling of cells. However, degassing is possible in pouch-type cells, such that the addition of sacrificial salts is likely to facilitate full cell configuration of the P2 cathode materials.

Sensitivity in air, in particular uptake of \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) in air, is a serious problem because of the formation of electrochemical-inactive \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\) on the surface of active materials. Ni-doping of the transition metal sites in \(\mathrm{Na}_x[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\) is less prone to react in air.[61]

2.1.3. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) and derivatives. Delmas et al. pioneered \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) intercalation properties using O3 type \(\mathrm{NaCoO}_2\). The structure underwent reversible structural transitions (O3 \(\leftrightarrow\) O'3 \(\leftrightarrow\) P'3) in the range of \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (\(x = 0 - 0.2\); Fig. 5a).[33] Apart from the excellent reversibility, the resulting capacity was very small in

comparison with P2 type \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (\(\sim 140\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) Delmas et al.).<sup>62</sup> Recent calculations revealed that direct hopping from one octahedral to an adjacent octahedral site requires high activation energy to overcome the barriers. In contrast, the P2 type framework has an open path for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) diffusion that is expected to have a lower diffusion barrier, such that \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) diffusion occurs readily in the P2 structure relative to the O3 structure. As mentioned in Section 2.1.1 and Fig. 3a and b, \(\mathrm{NaFeO}_2\) exhibits poor electrochemical performance. Again, \(\mathrm{NaCoO}_2\) is active in a very narrow range. However, once a solid solution of \(\mathrm{NaCoO}_2 - \mathrm{NaFeO}_2\) was formed, a high discharge capacity was obtained at high rates up to a 30C-rate, with \(\mathrm{Na[Fe_{0.5}Co_{0.5}]O_2}\) in particular.<sup>47</sup>

2.1.4. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CoO}_2(x\leq 0.3)\) and derivatives. Although sodium cobaltites can be used in thermoelectric and superconductor applications,[63-68] \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) is a \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion host material according to Delmas et al.[33] Due to the ease of synthesis, this material can be synthesized via a solid state reaction in the temperature range of \(500 - 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) under oxygen pressures of \(0.4\leq x\leq 0.45\) (P'3), \(0.26\leq x\leq 0.36\) (P2), \(x = 0.23\) (O'3), and \(x = 0\) (O3) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CoO}_2\). They suggested that oxygen-deficient \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_{2 - y}\) was a stabilized form due to the instability of \(\mathrm{Co}^{4 + }\) when \(\mathrm{Co}^{3 + }\) and \(\mathrm{Co}^{4 + }\) are mixed. Early work found that the P2 structure was maintained over a wide range \(0.46\leq x\leq 0.83\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\), although two-phase domains were observed in the charge-discharge curves (Fig. 5a).[33] Molenda et al. correlated discharge capacity and the oxygen content using \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{CoO}_{2 - y}\) (\(y = 0.004\) and 0.073; Fig. 5b).[69] The less-oxygen-deficient phase, \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{CoO}_{2 - y}\) (\(y = 0.004\)), resulted in more \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) intercalation than \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{CoO}_{2 - y}\) (\(y = 0.073\)) with a higher operating voltage. The original electron holes arising from oxygen nonstoichiometry lower the electric conductivity via formation of unstable \(\mathrm{Co}^{4 + }\). Hence, more Na should be added to equalize the Co oxidation state. This was proven experimentally by Chou et al. using the oxygen nonstoichiometric single crystal \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{CoO}_{2 - y}\) (\(y\sim 0.073\)) in air and \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.75}\mathrm{Co}_{2 - y}\) (\(y\sim 0.08\)) in oxygen.[70] For this reason, oxygen deficiency in \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) induces a lower electrochemical capacity and operating voltage such that an oxygen atmosphere is required to minimize oxygen deficiency and improve electrode performance in terms of capacity and operating voltage.

Delmas suggested the presence of several \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) structures and Shacklette confirmed that it has four phases. In particular, O3, \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\), and P3 layer structures were formed in a temperature range of \(400 - 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) whereas the P2 structure was stable only when the heating temperature was \(>700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) in an oxygen atmosphere via the conversion of the P3 phase, which involved rotation of \(\mathrm{CoO}_6\) octahedra and Co-O bond breakage. The voltage profile, operating voltage, and phase transition are very similar for the O3 and P3 phases during de-/sodiation (Fig. 5c). For the P2 phase, although similar behavior was observed in a voltage range of \(2.7 - 3.5\mathrm{V}\), phase transition appeared more complicated below \(2.7\mathrm{V}\) compared with O3 and P3 (Fig. 5c). The difference in \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)ordering for the P2 and P3 phases could account for the behavior variation below \(2.7\mathrm{V}\) at which considerable \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)is included at trigonal sites. Capacity retention was over \(80\%\) for 300 cycles for \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}CoO_2}\)

The higher capacity for the P2 phase may be due to its higher crystallinity compared with the P3 material produced at low temperatures. Ma et al. confirmed that \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}CoO_2}\) can be used for long-term cycling in a \(\mathrm{P(EO)_8NaCF_3SO_3}\) electrolyte at \(90^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\).

Berthelot et al. revisited \(\mathrm{P2 - Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) to investigate the phase diagram in a range of \(0.45\leq x\leq 0.9\), in which nine potential drops were found during the second discharge (Fig. 6a).<sup>62</sup> Weak polarization, which is an intrinsic property of \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}CoO_2}\) due to its high conductivity, enabled characterization of each voltage drop via in situ XRD. The (008) diffraction peak sometimes splits into two peaks, which is indicative of a two-phase domain when the discharge curve exhibits voltage plateaus, while the voltage drop indicates a strictly fixed peak due to the single phase reaction, demonstrating the presence of ordered phases \((\mathrm{Na}_{0.5}\mathrm{CoO}_2,\mathrm{Na}_{0.67}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (No. 3), \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.72}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (No. 7), \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.76}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (No. 8), and \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.79}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (No. 9), although they are present only in very narrow ranges. Between the voltage plateau and the voltage drop, the diffraction peak shifts slightly with solid solution behavior. Na ordering arises from two different repulsive interactions, sodium-sodium and sodium-cobalt. Occupation of Na at the shared edges \((\mathrm{Na_e})\) for \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}CoO_2}\) was predicted by first-principles calculations<sup>63,67</sup> and was

experimentally verified.\(^{62,68}\) At the same time, some of the Na ions are located at the shared faces \((\mathrm{Na_f})\). For \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.5}CoO_2}\), an ordered phase, half of Na occupies \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) sites while the rest appears at \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) sites, which probably minimizes the repulsive in-plane interaction between \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_e}\). The distribution of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ions in these two positions depends on the Na content in the compound, which affects the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}/\)vacancy-ordered structure.

Croguennec et al. obtained \(\mathrm{T}^{\# 2}\mathrm{-Li}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Co}_{2 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) via ion-exchange from \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Co_{2 / 3}Mn_{1 / 3}]O_2}\), in which the oxidation states of Co and Mn were \(3+\) and \(4+\), respectively.\(^{73,74}\) Cheng et al. also confirmed that the oxidation states of Co and Mn for \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Co_{2 / 3}Mn_{1 / 3}]O_2}\) are stabilized at \(3+\) and \(4+\), respectively.\(^{75}\) Carlier et al.\(^{76}\) evaluated \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Co_{2 / 3}Mn_{1 / 3}]O_2}\) in the Na cell and Yang et al.\(^{77}\) extended the stability of \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Co_{1 - x}Mn_x]O_2}\) to \(x = 0.5\). The resulting \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Co_{2 / 3}Mn_{1 / 3}]O_2}\) shows suppression of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}/\)vacancy ordering in the structure because the stepwise voltage plateau disappeared in a range of \(0.5 \leq x \leq 0.83\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x[\mathrm{Co}_{2 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2 / 3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) (Fig. 6b). Note that \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.74}CoO_2}\) formed nine distinct phases in a range of \(0.5 \leq x \leq 0.9\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) (Fig. 6a). However, the single phase domain for \(x = 0.5\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) is still found in \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Co}_{2 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 3}]\mathrm{O}_2\), although the voltage drop at that composition is lower than in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\). The main difference after Mn substitution for Co is abrupt voltage decay in a range of \(0.65 \leq x \leq 0.83\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x[\mathrm{Co}_{2 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 3}]\mathrm{O}_2\). They further

Yang et al.\(^{77}\) successfully stabilized the \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Co_{1 - x}Mn_x]O_2}\) phase to \(x = 0.5\), although the resulting capacity was limited to \(120\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\). Meanwhile, Co\(^{3 + / 2 + }\) and Mn\(^{4 + / 3 + }\) reactions

were more dominant in a voltage range of \(1.5 - 2.1\mathrm{V}\). Even at high rates, half capacity was delivered in the voltage range of \(1.5 - 2.1\mathrm{V}\) associated with the \(\mathrm{Co}^{3 + / 2 + }\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + / 3 + }\) reactions. The structural stabilization achieved in the biphasic compound may explain the excellent electrochemical performance. However, the capacity obtained in the low voltage region may decrease the energy density as use these electrode materials for cathodes.

Recently, Matsui et al. explored the possibility of Ca-doping at Na sites to form \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3-x}\mathrm{Ca}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\). The similarity in the ionic radius of \(\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\) (1.00 Å) versus \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) (1.02 Å) allows the incorporation of \(\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\) into the Na sites. Although the delivered capacity decreased to some extent due to \(\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\) in the Na layers, \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3-x}\mathrm{Ca}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) could suppress the multiple phase transition during charge and discharge. For example, \(\mathrm{Na}_{5/8}\mathrm{Ca}_{1/24}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) could be cycled even at \(5\mathrm{mA~cm}^{-1}\) with very little capacity decay, while capacity fade was inevitable in Ca-free \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3-x}\mathrm{CoO}_2\). Post-cycled electrodes showed formation of a sodium poor phase of \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}\mathrm{CoO}_2\), while \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3-x}\mathrm{Ca}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) suppressed this phase separation. This work emphasizes that stability in the Na environment is another topic of investigation that will contribute to development of a long-term cyclable cathode for Na cells.

Xia et al. tested the reactivity of desodiated \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.35}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) derived from \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.65}CoO_2}\) in the \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\)-based electrolyte.[80] \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.35}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) decomposed to \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{CoO}_2\) and \(\mathrm{Co}_3\mathrm{O}_4\) with oxygen release from the crystal structure. The \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\) salt rapidly reacted with \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.35}\mathrm{CoO}_2\), and \(\mathrm{NaCoF_3}\) was produced via the exothermic reaction. This reaction has not been reported in a \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) system thus far. Selection of the electrolytic salt is another important issue to utilize the electrode in Na cells.

2.1.5. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{NiO}_2\) and derivatives. \(\mathrm{NaNiO_2}\) is stable as two polymorphs, a low temperature type with an O3 layer structure and a high temperature rhombohedral phase. Similar to \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{NaFeO_2}\), the Ni-O layer shares edges between \(\mathrm{NiO_6}\) octahedra in which the Jahn-Teller \(\mathrm{Ni}^{3 + }\) ions are elongated.[81] Since the starting material, Ni, is divalent, an oxidative environment is needed to synthesize \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\) type \(\mathrm{NaNiO_2}\) with the space group \(C2 / m\). Early work reported by Braconnier et al. suggested that \(0.2\mathrm{mol}\) of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\), which has approximately \(50~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) of capacity, was deintercalated from \(\mathrm{NaNiO_2}\) via \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\leftrightarrow \mathrm{P}^{\prime}3\leftrightarrow\) \(\mathrm{P}^{\prime}3\leftrightarrow \mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\) multiple phase transition based on the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox reaction in the voltage range of \(1.7 - 3.5\mathrm{V}\).[33] Later work by Vassilaras reported delivery of a high capacity of approximately \(145\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) in a \(2.2 - 4.5\mathrm{V}\) voltage range.[81,82] By charging to \(4.5\mathrm{V}\), however, the capacity retention stabilized when the upper voltage cutoff was lowered to \(3.75\mathrm{V}\), with \(94\%\) of the initial capacity (\(115\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\)) after 20 cycles. The considerable Coulombic efficiency was due to oxidation of the electrolyte when charged to high voltage (Fig. 7a).

Komaba et al. applied the findings of research on \(\mathrm{Li}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\) to \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\), in which the average oxidation states of Ni and Mn are \(2+\) and \(4+\), respectively.[83] The material was also crystallized into an O3-type layer structure and could be solely activated by the redox reaction of \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+/4+}\). In comparison with \(\mathrm{NaNiO}_2\), the presence of tetravalent Mn induced the formation of \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) in \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\). The two-electron reaction led to a

high discharge capacity of \(185\mathrm{mA}\) h \(\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in a voltage range of \(2.5 - 4.5\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 7b), whereas optimization of the electrode performance limited the upper voltage cutoff to \(3.8\mathrm{V}\), resulting in a capacity above \(100\mathrm{mA}\) h \(\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) over 20 cycles. In contrast to \(\mathrm{NaNiO}_2\), the \(\mathrm{O3}\leftrightarrow \mathrm{O}'3\leftrightarrow \mathrm{P3}\leftrightarrow \mathrm{P}'3\) phase transition was highly reversible in a voltage range of \(2.5 - 3.8\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 7c). Fe-substituted \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\), however, underwent different structural transformation during desodiation compared with \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\).[84] Phase evolution from P3 to \(\mathrm{P3''}\) was predominant in \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\), whereas \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)extraction from Fe-substituted \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2\) facilitated a gradual evolution from P3 to OP2 due to the migration of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\) ions from the octahedron of the transition metal layers to the interstitial tetrahedron or the octahedron of the Na layers (Fig. 7d). Nonetheless, capacity retention was improved in a voltage range of \(2 - 4.3\mathrm{V}\). In particular, a long irreversible reaction due to the formation of \(\mathrm{P3''}\) phase transitioned to the OP2 phase in a voltage range of \(4 - 4.3\mathrm{V}\) on charge, although the length of the plateau did not appear on discharge. This peculiar behavior seems to be related to the effect of Fe. Recently, Hwang et al. investigated the collation of Ni and Fe contents in \(\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.75 - x}\mathrm{Fe}_x\mathrm{Mn}_{0.25}]\mathrm{O}_2\) (\(x = 0.4, 0.45, 0.5\), and \(0.55\)).[85] The structural and thermal stabilities, which affect cycle retention and rate capability, were dependent on the Fe content. Although the discharge capacity of higher Fe content materials decreased slightly, the improved cycling performance and rate capability compensate for the slightly reduced capacity. The increase in the Fe content seems to improve conductivity, which is associated with low band gap energy (\(\sim 2.5\mathrm{eV}\) for \(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_3\).[86] Therefore, enhanced rate performance with increasing Fe content is attributed to the improvement in electric conductivity derived from Fe in the compound. Also, their DSC study revealed that reactive \(\mathrm{Ni}^{4+}\) ions in the desodiated host material cause oxygen removal from the crystal structure, and that oxygen evolution can effectively suppress the Fe increase in the crystal structure (Fig. 7e).

They also developed a radially-aligned hierarchical columnar structure in spherical particles with a varied chemical composition from the inner end \(\mathrm{(Na[Ni_{0.75}Co_{0.02}Mn_{0.23}]O_2)}\) to the outer end \(\mathrm{(Na[Ni_{0.58}Co_{0.06}Mn_{0.36}]O_2)}\) of the structure.[87] An electrochemical reaction based on \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+ / 3+ / 4+}\) had a discharge capacity of \(157\mathrm{mA}\) h \((\mathrm{g}\cdot\mathrm{oxide})^{-1}\) with a capacity retention of \(80\%\) \((125\mathrm{mA}\) h \(\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) ) over 300 cycles in combination with a hard carbon anode. The cathode also exhibited good temperature performance, even at \(-20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), which enables the Ni redox reaction.

Ti-Substituted \(\mathrm{O3 - Na[Ni_{0.5}Ti_{0.5}]O_2}\) is also interesting due to its superior cyclability under moderate conditions, with an average operating potential of \(3.1\mathrm{V}\) (vs. \(\mathrm{Na^{+} / Na}\)) and delivery of a reversible capacity of \(121\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at \(20\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\). Since the average oxidation states of Ni and Ti are \(2+\) and \(4+\), respectively, the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + / 4 + }\) redox reaction is responsible for electrochemical activity.[88] Increasing the Ni content resulted in higher capacity, while a dramatic degradation in capacity and thermal properties was observed. In contrast, increasing the Fe content improved capacity retention and thermal stability in a highly desodiated state. Hence, it is worth noting that Ni redox such as \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + / 4 + }\) or

2.1.6. \(\mathbf{Na}_x\mathbf{MnO}_2\) and derivatives. In comparison to the other compounds, these compounds are in particular interesting

because of the cost effectiveness of the Na and Mn elements. \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (\(x = 0.44 - 1\)) have been extensively investigated as cathode materials since the first report by Parant et al.[89] These structures are three-dimensional at lower \(x\) values (\(x = 0 - 0.44\)) and two-dimensional at higher \(x\) values (\(x > 0.5\)). sodium content, \(\alpha\)-NaMnO\(_2\) and \(\beta\)-NaMnO\(_2\) are stable, although the phase stability of both compounds is dependent on temperature; for example, \(\alpha\)-NaMnO\(_2\) (space group: \(C2/m\)) is the low-temperature form and \(\beta\)-NaMnO\(_2\) (space group: Pmm) is the high-temperature form. P2-\(Na_{0.7}MnO_2\) is no more stable when the Na layer is fully sodiated to \(\mathrm{NaMnO}_2\) at a low temperature. In a P2 layer structure, \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) sites are simultaneously occupied, due to the strong sodium-sodium repulsion interaction in the Na layers (Fig. 2). Hence, simultaneous distribution of \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) is not possible in the P2 phase, but all \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions are located in one site to form \(\alpha\)-NaMnO\(_2\) (space group: \(C2/m\), O'3 structure, Fig. 8a). The prime symbol is an indication of a monoclinic structure with respect to the hexagonal lattice. Since the average oxidation state of Mn is \(3+\), the Jahn-Teller distortion prevails in the crystal structure.

Ma and Ceder et al. reported that monoclinic \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\mathrm{-NaMnO}_{2}\) could deliver charge and discharge capacities of approximately \(210\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) and \(197\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), respectively, in a voltage range of \(2 - 3.8\mathrm{V}\) and at a rate of C/30 (Fig. 8b). This behavior differs from an earlier report by Mendiboure and Hagenmuller. The charge and discharge curves show many plateaus and voltage drops, with eight and five charge and discharge plateaus, respectively. The hysteresis was reversible even after cycling. A long plateau observed at \(2.63\mathrm{V}\) from \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.93}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) to \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) is associated with a two-phase reaction. Their ex situ XRD investigation revealed that the second phase was \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{MnO}_2\), although this was not consistent with orthorhombic \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (space group: Cmcm) or P2- \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (space group: P63/mmc). Capacity retention of \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\mathrm{-NaMnO}_2\) was approximately \(74\%\) for 10 cycles without a significant structural change compared to the fresh electrode. In \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\)-containing cathode materials, capacity fade is usually mentioned with Mn dissolution, which causes disproportionation to \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\). Only a small percentage of Mn was dissolved from the active material (less than \(32~\mu\mathrm{g}\) from \(2 - 3\mathrm{mg}\) of the active material), such that dissolution was not likely to affect electrode performance. Their successive work demonstrated the readiness of the \(180^{\circ}\mathrm{Na}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Na}\) strip formation in contrast to \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Na}}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Na}\), albeit insufficient \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)ions in \(\mathrm{Na}_{5/8}\mathrm{MnO}_2\), because \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)ions relax to the highly distorted octahedral sites, where they share the symmetric attraction of two neighboring Jahn-Teller distorted \(-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Na}\) configurations along the [100] axis. This structural imperfection may be the reason for the gradual capacity fade observed during cycling in this system.

High temperature-type \(\beta\)-\mathrm{NaMnO}_2\) has a zigzag layer structure composed of two edge-sharing stacks of the \(\mathrm{MnO}_6\) octahedra. Between two neighboring sheets, the sodium ions occupy octahedral sites.[92] In the Li system, orthorhombic \(\mathrm{LiMnO}_2\) is directly synthesized, indicating that the orthorhombic structure is energetically favored relative to monoclinic \(\mathrm{LiMnO}_2\), which is usually produced via ion exchange from \(\alpha\)-\mathrm{NaMnO}_2\). In the Na system, however, first principles calculations indicate that the monoclinic \(\alpha\)-\mathrm{NaMnO}_2\) is energetically more stable than orthorhombic \(\beta\)-\mathrm{NaMnO}_2.[93,94] \(\beta\)-\mathrm{NaMnO}_2\) is active in a narrow range \((0.85 \leq x \leq 0.96)\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\).[91] In this range, a two-phase domain is attributed to \(\beta\)-\mathrm{NaMnO}_2\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\).

Phase transformation towards spinel was possible in \(\beta\)-LiMnO\(_2\), because Mn can migrate into tetrahedral Li sites to form the spinel phase. This phase transition is unlikely to occur because tetrahedral sites rarely form in spinel with Na due to the large ionic size of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) relative to \(\mathrm{Li}^+\).

Reducing the Na content to 0.7 induces formation of a different crystal structure: P2-, P3, and orthorhombic \((\mathrm{P}^{\prime}2)\) \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (Fig. 8c).<sup>95</sup> As mentioned in Section 2.1, P3 is the low temperature type and P2 is the high temperature type (Fig. 2). Stoyanova found that orthorhombic \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.7}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (space group: Cmcm) is stable at \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) \((\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}_{3/2}\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}_{1/3}]\mathrm{O}_2)\)<sup>95,96</sup>

Among these polymorphs, \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}MnO_2}\) and its derivatives have been intensively studied.[97-99] An early report by Caballero et al. showed reversible capacity delivery of more than \(150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in a voltage range of 2-3.8 V in Na cells.[98] They also suggested that intercalation of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions occurred in several steps, but the resulting capacity fade progressed upon successive cycling tests. Increasing the synthetic temperature to \(900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), Yabuuchi et al. could improve the capacity up to \(190\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in a voltage range of 1.5-4.3 V during several early cycles (Fig. 8d).[99,100] The electrode performance is similar to that of \(\mathrm{O3^{\prime} - NaMnO_2}\), although the related phase transition is different.

\(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Ni^{2 + }_{1 / 3}Mn^{4 + }_{2 / 3}]O_2}\) was first reported in 2001 by Lu and Dahn et al. This compound is stable in moist air, and hydration via the insertion of water molecules does not occur. Although it has a relatively low theoretical capacity \((173\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Ni^{2 + }_{1 / 3}Mn^{4 + }_{2 / 3}]O_2}\) showed an average operating voltage of \(3.5\mathrm{V}\) with a \(\mathrm{Ni^{2 + } / 4^{+}}\) redox reaction, delivering approximately \(160~\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in a voltage range of \(2 - 4.5\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 8e). Because of the similarity in the ionic size between \(\mathrm{Ni^{2 + }}\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + }\), \(\mathrm{Ni^{2 + }}\) prefers to occupy the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + }\) sites instead of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + }\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}\mathrm{MnO}_2\). Their in situ XRD study revealed a reversible P2-O2 phase transition stemming from the oxygen shift, in which the O2 phase prevails at a voltage plateau above \(4\mathrm{V}\) on charge and \(3.8\mathrm{V}\) on discharge (Fig. 8f). This is the main difference from \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.67}MnO_2}\); the absence of the Jahn-Teller distortion \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + }\) is responsible for the occurrence of the P2-O2 phase transition. This transition is associated with a large volume change when the O2 phase appears. Recently, Meng et al. revisited \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Ni_{1 / 3}Mn_{2 / 3}]O_2}\) and observed phase transformation from P2 to O2 at \(4.2\mathrm{V}\). The long voltage plateau is the evidence of two forms of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion ordering: one row of \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) and two rows of \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) in \(\mathrm{Na_{1 / 2}[Ni_{1 / 3}Mn_{2 / 3}]O_2}\) and Na orders in rows on either \(\mathrm{Na_e}\) or \(\mathrm{Na_f}\) order in \(\mathrm{Na_{1 / 3}[Ni_{1 / 3}Mn_{2 / 3}]O_2}\), in which the latter corresponds to the region where the O2 phase is dominant. In the P2 phase, the path with the minimum energy passes through a shared face between two neighboring Na prismatic sites. Here, \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions need around \(170\mathrm{meV}\) for diffusion in the P2 phase. In the O2 phase, \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions cross the tetrahedron between two octahedral sites by means of a vacancy mechanism. The required energy for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) diffusion in the O2 phase is \(290\mathrm{meV}\), indicating slow \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) mobility in the O2 phase, as was confirmed experimentally. It is reasonable because the diffusion path of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions is more spacious in the P2 phase relative to the O2 phase; this leads to a lower activation barrier (Fig. 8e inset). Hence, a cycling test in the voltage range of \(2.3 - 4.1\mathrm{V},\)

P2-O2 transition up to \(4.4\mathrm{V}\) on charge, showing a simple solid solution reaction across the entire range.\(^{105}\) As a result, there was no characteristic voltage plateau in a voltage range of \(4.1 - 4.4\mathrm{V}\). This feature ensured excellent capacity retention as high as \(91\%\) for 50 cycles (\(\sim 120\mathrm{mA}\) \(\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at the first discharge).

Substitution of divalent elements such as \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) in Ni is effective for diluting the effect of the above-mentioned P2-O2 phase transition.\(^{106-113}\) A decrease in the initial charge capacity is natural because the electro-active species \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) was reduced by substitution, as shown in Fig. 9a, which reflects the origination of the capacity drop from the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+/4+}\) redox reaction. Stepwise voltage plateaus were not dominant throughout the operation range of \(2-4.5\mathrm{~V}\).\(^{106,107}\) Compared with \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\), the capacity drop in \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.67}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.2}\mathrm{Mg}_{0.1}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.7}]\mathrm{O}_2\) was only \(6\mathrm{~mA~h~g}^{-1}\) for 50 cycles.\(^{107}\) Using in situ XRD, instead of the P2 phase, a new phase identified as an OP4 phase was found at voltages above \(4.2\mathrm{~V}\), where a short voltage plateau was observed compared to \(\mathrm{Mg}\)-free \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Mn}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) (Fig. 9b). The presence of electro-inactive \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) in transition metal layers facilitates occupation of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions in prismatic sites, stabilizing the overall charge balance of the compounds. Since the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions are less extracted from the host structure assisted by \(\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\) in the transition metal layers, the original P2 phase is retained as the major phase, though transition to OP4 because deep desodiation is observed at high voltage as a minor phase. Hence, the P2-O2 transition and the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\)/vacancy ordering in \(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3-x}\mathrm{Mg}_x\mathrm{Mn}_{0.7}]\mathrm{O}_2\) are suppressed during cycling via Mg substitution. This P2-OP4 phase transition is highly reversible; the cycling performance demonstrates its superiority compared to the P2-O2 phase transition.\(^{101}\) While Zn-substituted P2-\(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}-[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3-x}\mathrm{Zn}_x\mathrm{Mn}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) induces reversible phase transition during electrochemical reactions.\(^{111,112}\) Although the capacity was still limited to approximately \(140\mathrm{~mA~h~g}^{-1}\) by the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+/4+}\) redox reaction due to reductions in electro-active \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\), the enhanced structural integrity achieved by \(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\) in the transition metal layer enabled good cyclability due to readiness for transformation and a less variation in unit volume. Further studies explored partial replacement of Ni sites by Al.\(^{113}\) The substitution displayed sloping charge-discharge curves due to the diminution of the electro-active \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) concentration, suggesting that \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+/4+}\) redox has superior capacity delivery. The substituent stabilized the host structure and the resulting cyclability was significantly improved, in particular for Al-doped P2-\(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\), which had \(94.8\%\) (from \(\sim 147\mathrm{~mA~h~g}^{-1}\)) retention over 30 cycles.

Replacement with a trivalent 3d transition metal element such as Co and Fe is also interesting because they are electrochemically active in the same operating range.\(^{114-116}\) Yoshida et al. investigated the composition of \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}[Mn_{0.6}Ni_{0.3}Co_{0.1}]O_2}\), in which the oxidation state of Mn is approximately \(3.4+\), in a voltage range of \(1.5 - 4.3\mathrm{V}\). Note that the oxidation state of Mn is \(4+\) in \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Ni_{1 / 3}Mn_{2 / 3}]O_2}\). As expected, the material exhibited multiple voltage plateaus. In comparison with \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2 / 3}]O_2}\), the improvement in capacity during the first cycle (approximately \(204\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)) is due to the additional capacity contributed by \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox below \(4\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 9c). The long plateau above \(4\mathrm{V}\) confirmed the validity of the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + / 4 + }\) redox reaction.

As cycles went by, the length of the upper voltage plateau related to \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+ / 4+}\) and the lower voltage plateaus related to \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+ / 4+}\) \(2\mathrm{V}\) decreased. This can be interpreted in two ways: (1) the progressive formation of the O2 phase at high voltage and the effect of the Jahn-Teller distortion in the crystal lattice. This further limited the cycling region to three parts: \(1.5 - 4.3\mathrm{V}\), \(1.5 - 4.0\mathrm{V}\), and \(1.7 - 4.0\mathrm{V}\). It is evident that, although the Jahn-Teller distortion is present in low voltage regions, severe capacity fade occurs in high voltage regions. This explains that the P2-O2 phase transition is not favored when maintaining the capacity because of the large difference in the \(c\)-axis, which causes a drop in the capacity (Fig. 9d). Jahn-Teller distortion seems to be less pronounced in capacity retention. In contrast, instead of Co, Yuan et al. suggested that Ni substitution with Fe \((\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.7}[Mn_{0.65}Ni_{0.15}Fe_{0.2}]O_2})\) led to an improvement in capacity and retention: \(208\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with \(71\%\) retention over 50 cycles.[115] The phase transition was P2 to OP4 in this case.[117]

Tetravalent Ti-substituted \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Ni_{1 / 3}Mn_{2 / 3 - x}Ti_x]O_2}\) yielded a wide solid solution range of \(0\leq x\leq 2 / 3\) .118,119 Due to the similarity of both \(\mathrm{Mn^{4 + }}\) and \(\mathrm{Ti^{4 + }}\) in the valence and ionic radius, such a solid solution can be formed readily throughout the range. The reason remains unclear, but an increase in the Ti content resulted in a decrease in capacity. In particular, \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2 / 3 - x}\mathrm{Ti}_x]\mathrm{O}_2(x = 1 / 6)\) delivered approximately \(127\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with an average operating voltage of approximately \(3.7\mathrm{V}\) on discharge, which corresponds to \(470\mathrm{W}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{kg}^{-1}\) . More importantly, the stepwise voltage profiles of \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1 / 3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2 / 3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) were obviously diluted by Ti substitution. XRD suggested that the volume change of the fully charged state was reduced from \(23.1\%\) in Ti-free compounds to \(12 - 13\%\) for Ti-substituted compounds, confirming the suppression of Na/vacancy ordering and phase transitions during sodium intercalation.

A new mechanism was introduced in \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{2 / 3}[Mg_{0.28}Mn_{0.72}]O_2}\) and \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{5 / 6}[Li_{1 / 4}Mn_{3 / 4}]O_2}\) by Yabuuchi and Komaba et al.\(^{120,121}\) In this compound, since \(\mathrm{Mg^{2 + }}\) and \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) are electrochemically inactive, the only possible reaction should be associated with Mn redox upon de-/sodiation. While the average oxidation states of Mn are \(3.84+\) for \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Mg}_{0.28}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.72}]\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(3.88+\) for \(\mathrm{Na}_{5 / 6}[\mathrm{Li}_{1 / 4}\mathrm{Mn}_{3 / 4}]\mathrm{O}_2\). Nonetheless, both materials exhibited large capacities of approximately \(220\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) for \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}[\mathrm{Mg}_{0.28}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.72}]\mathrm{O}_2\) (Fig. 9e) and \(180\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) for \(\mathrm{Na}_{5 / 6}[\mathrm{Li}_{1 / 4}\mathrm{Mn}_{3 / 4}]\mathrm{O}_2\). Similarly, a recent report by Slater et al. also showed a large capacity of \(200\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) for \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.85}[Li_{0.17}Ni_{0.21}Mn_{0.64}]O_2}\). Taking the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox species into account, it is not possible to explain the delivery of high capacity. Indeed, there is almost no delivery of capacity in the voltage cutoff up to \(4\mathrm{V}\) on charge; however, those capacities are delivered above \(4\mathrm{V}\), which is related to oxidation of oxide ions, resulting in the release of oxygen from the oxide lattice. Oxygen removal causes formation of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + }\), which simultaneously participates in oxidation on charge and is reduced on discharge. This causes rearrangement of the in-plane cation ordering of the crystal structure, which is similar to a Li-rich \(\mathrm{Li_2MnO_3}\) system.\(^{122}\) P2-O2 transition was also observed in these compounds (Fig. 9f), which is worth mentioning because of the theoretical capacity of \(173\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) based on the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + / 4 + }\) redox reaction. \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + }\) was successfully overcome by the oxygen compensation accompanied by the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox reaction.

facilitate the formation of \(\mathrm{Al_2O_3}\)-coated P2-\(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\), although there is some possibility of Al doping in the crystal structure because additional heating after the removal of the aqueous medium was performed at \(650~^\circ\mathrm{C}\) for \(10\mathrm{h}\). \(\mathrm{Al_2O_3}\)-coated P2-\(\mathrm{Na}_{2/3}[\mathrm{Ni}_{1/3}\mathrm{Mn}_{2/3}]\mathrm{O}_2\) had good cycling performance for 300 cycles with approximately \(72\%\) retention in a voltage range of \(2.5-4.3\mathrm{V}\). Although the detailed mechanism related to this high capacity retention remains unclear, structural stabilization of the host material by the coating was suspected.

2.1.7. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{TiO}_2\) and derivatives. \(\mathrm{NaTiO_2}\) was first synthesized by Hagenmuller et al. and Maazaz et al. evaluated its potential as a \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion/extraction material.\(^{124,125}\) This material underwent phase transformation from \(\mathrm{O3} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{O}'3\), in which the chemical composition reaches \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}TiO_2}\) (approximately \(75\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)) on desodiation, showing an average operating voltage of \(1\mathrm{V}\), which is suitable for an anode. In contrast to the other materials, the electrode exhibited a low operating voltage of \(\sim 1\mathrm{V}\), followed by the \(\mathrm{Ti}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox reaction. When a solid solution is formed with \(\mathrm{NaNiO_2}\) \((\mathrm{Na}[\mathrm{Ni}_{0.5}\mathrm{Ti}_{0.5}]\mathrm{O}_2)\) as suggested above by Yu et al., the material is sufficiently stable for long-term cycling as a cathode because Ti enables significant structural stability and the \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + / 4 + }\) redox couple contributes to capacity delivery.\(^{88}\)

2.1.8. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CrO}_2\) and derivatives. The first study of \(\mathrm{O3 - NaCrO_2}\) showed limited capacity, which was desodiated to \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.85}\mathrm{CrO}_2\).<sup>126</sup> Miyazaki et al. also investigated \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) desodiation via chemical and electrochemical methods, in which extraction facilitated the formation of \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.4}\mathrm{CrO}_2\).<sup>127</sup> They also confirmed the formation of \(\mathrm{Cr^{4 + }}\) for desodiated \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.5}\mathrm{CrO}_2\), as analyzed based on magnetic susceptibility. Recently, Komaba et al. revisited O3 type layer-structured \(\mathrm{NaCrO}_2\), in which Na ions could be inserted into/extracted from the host structure.<sup>128</sup> In contrast to \(\mathrm{LiCrO}_2\) in Li cells, \(\mathrm{NaCrO}_2\) could deliver a capacity of approximately \(110\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in the voltage range of 2–3.6 V due to the greater inter-slab distance provided by the presence of large \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions in the Na layers. Despite the high theoretical capacity of about \(250\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), the practical reversible capacity was approximately \(110\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\left(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CrO}_2,0\leq x\leq 0.5\right)\), with a flattened voltage plateau at \(3\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 10a). Dahn's group also reported that desodiated \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.5}\mathrm{CrO}_2\) has excellent thermal stability in Na-based non-aqueous electrolytes.<sup>129</sup> However, \(\mathrm{NaCrO}_2\) electrodes suffer from capacity fading during cycling.<sup>128,129</sup>

In consideration of synthetic conditions, \(\mathrm{NaCrO_2}\) is usually produced in a reducing atmosphere to retain the oxidation state of Cr at \(3+\). This condition enables carbon coating, which can dramatically improve electrode performance. Ding et al. found that citric acid-assisted carbon coating slightly improved the cycling stability of \(\mathrm{NaCrO_2}\) electrodes.[130] Although the cycling performance of carbon-coated \(\mathrm{NaCrO_2}\) was improved compared to the bare material, operation at high rates was not possible, presumably due to inhomogeneous or excessively thick carbon layers. The XANES study revealed that \(\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\) is oxidized to a higher chemical state on charge. The above results are not satisfactory for use in practical applications because of rapid capacity fading (ca. \(80\%\) in the 50th cycle) and disappointing rate capability. Yu et al. modified the surfaces of the \(\mathrm{NaCrO_2}\)

particles via carbonization of pitch, which improved the electrical conductivity to approximately \(10^{-1}\) S cm\(^{-1}\). The carbon-coated \(\mathrm{NaCrO}_2\) electrode exhibited excellent cyclability and an ultrafast rate capability of up to a 150C-rate (Fig. 10b). High electrical conductivity successfully promotes reversible insertion and extraction of sodium ions accompanied by a facile complementary redox reaction of the \(\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}/\mathrm{Cr}^{4+}\) couple as confirmed by XANES (Fig. 10c-e). The \(\mathrm{NaCrO}_2\) electrode was also sufficiently stable in an intermediate temperature ionic liquid, NaFSA-KFSA, at 363 K.\(^{132}\) Excellent electrochemical performance of these cathodes was demonstrated in both half and full sodium ion cells. Apart from the electrode performance, the carbon coating layer also delays exothermic decomposition by preventing oxygen evaporation from the crystal lattice; specifically, the lower heat generation from the desodiated \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.5}\mathrm{CrO}_2\) was ascribed to the suppression of oxygen evolution during the exothermic reaction, which results from the presence of carbon coating layers.

A major challenge associated with sodiated cathode materials is immediate water absorption after exposure to air. This leads to the formation of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}\) on the surface of active materials. The sodium diffuses to the surface, and electrochemically inactive parts are formed within the particles. Even worse, the \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) and \(\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}\) formed are electrical insulators. The carbon coating can prevent moisture uptake due to its hydrophobic characteristics.

2.1.9. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{VO}_2\) and derivatives. Among several \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{VO}_2\) compounds, only two compositions, \(x = 0\) and \(x = 0.3\), have been identified;[133,134] the former represents O3 and the latter, P2. However, \(\mathrm{O3 - NaVO_2}\) reacts in air for a few seconds, leading to desodiated \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{VO}_2\) phases.[134] \(\mathrm{NaVO_2}\) shows highly reversible \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction in a voltage range of \(1.2 - 2.4\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 11a). The first charge plateau is a typical characteristic of the biphasic domain until the composition reaches \(\mathrm{Na}_{2 / 3}\mathrm{VO}_2\). Complicated behavior is observed upon further sodiation, showing three voltage plateaus. Approximately \(120\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) was delivered. Upon desodiation, the O3 structure transforms into the \(\mathrm{O}^{\prime}3\) structure via monoclinic distortion. \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)extraction above \(x = 0.5\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{VO}_2\) deteriorates electrode performance, resulting from the migration of vanadium ions into interslab vacancies. This phenomenon is similar to \(\mathrm{Na}_{1 - x}\mathrm{CrO}_2\).[128,130] Despite considerable voltage variation and oxygen sensitivity, capacity fading was negligible for 15 cycles, as reported by Hamani et al.[135] In \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0,7}VO_2}\), the resulting charge and discharge behavior is very reversible in a voltage range of \(1.2 - 2.6\mathrm{V}\), delivering approximately \(105\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) (Fig. 11b). In contrast to the Fe- and Mn-based compounds presented in Sections 2.1.2 and 2.1.6, the related phase transition is more or less complicated (Fig. 11c and d). An abrupt voltage drop occurs due to the single phase domain and the plateaus are associated with the solid solution reaction. Hence, four single phase domains and solid solutions are present between the single phase domains, which have been identified in XRD studies.[136] Similarly, these multi-domains are also observed in \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_xCoO_2}\).[62] Since \(\mathrm{NaVO_2}\) is stable in a reducing atmosphere, carbon coating of \(\mathrm{NaVO_2}\) could further minimize oxygen uptake in air. Successful carbon

2.1.10. \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{RuO}_3\). The \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{MO}_3\) (M: metal) layer structure, an analogue of \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{MO}_3\), has alternative layers in a cubic close-packed oxygen array. \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{RuO}_3\) shows metallic conduction and is crystallized in layer structures consisting of Na and \(\mathrm{Na}_{1/3}\mathrm{Ru}_{2/3}\) slabs.[137] The crystal structure is indexed as hexagonal \(R\bar{3}m\) without ordering of the superstructure, unlike \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{MnO}_3\). The first discharge capacity was approximately \(150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at the first cycle and this capacity was maintained throughout the cycling test. For \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction, the electrochemical

reaction proceeded via a two-phase reaction with hex-I and hex-II phases. Although further studies are needed to elucidate the structural evolution during \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion and extraction, the use of layered \(\mathrm{Na_2MO_3}\) is an important approach for exploring new transition metal oxides.
2.2. Two- or three-dimensional layer transition metal oxides and fluorides

Na-deficiency or Na-free transition metal oxides are interesting because of their reversible \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction. These compounds usually have an open structure to allow \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions into their crystal structure. Particularly in three-dimensional
