Since the ionic size of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) is larger than that of \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\), the host structure should be sufficiently rigid or have a large tunnel size to facilitate the entry of large ionic species. Otherwise, the crystal structure could collapse upon repetitive \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion and extraction. These electrodes are active compared to Na metal; however, the main difficulty is that a sodiated anode is needed to construct a full cell. Recent research introduced low voltage operating \(\mathrm{P2 - Na_{0.62}[\mathrm{Ti}_{0.37}\mathrm{Cr}_{0.63}]O_2}\) anode materials.\(^{138}\) Plenty of possibilities remain regarding the development of full cells utilizing these transition metal oxides and sodiated anode materials.

2.2.1. Manganese oxides. Several types of Na-Mn-O compounds were introduced by Parant et al.[89,90] A lower Na/Mn ratio results in adoption of a three-dimensional structure such as \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.2}\mathrm{MnO}_2\),[90] \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.4}\mathrm{MnO}_2\),[89] or \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) (space group \(Pbam\))[89,90,139-145] Among these, \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\), which is isostructural

with \(\mathrm{Na}_4\mathrm{Mn}_4\mathrm{Ti}_5\mathrm{O}_{18}\), is particularly interesting because of its cycling stability with a reasonable capacity, \(\sim 120\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\). Doeff et al. first examined the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion properties of \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) with a solid-state polymer electrolyte at \(85^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The crystal structure consists of four \(\mathrm{MnO}_6\) octahedral sites with \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\) and one \(\mathrm{MnO}_5\) square-pyramidal site with a half of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\) (Fig. 12a). These are connected by corner sharing to form two types of tunnels. Each unit cell contains a large S-shaped tunnel with four sodium sites, as well as two identical pentagonal tunnels. The Na sites in the small tunnels are nearly fully occupied, while the large tunnel sites are partially occupied: Na1 and 2 sites in the S-shaped tunnels, half-filled; Na3 sites with an inner position in the small tunnels, fully-filled. Both \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions are highly mobile along the \(c\)-axis, contributing to capacity. Sauvage et al. performed carbon coating of \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\), which led to a capacity of \(140\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\). They speculated

that the Na1 and Na2 sites located in the S-shaped tunnels were very accessible in the range of 0.22 to 0.66 in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\), while the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions located in the Na3 site were not generally extracted. An in situ XRD study revealed the structural evolution during the electrochemical reaction in Na cells. A biphasic reaction was found upon reduction in the range of \(x = 0.2 - 0.44\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\). In a range of \(x = 0.44 - 0.612\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\), not a single solid solution, but several multiphase reactions were associated with the electrochemical oxidation. Unfortunately, the high rate test caused a drastic capacity drop (to \(\sim 10^{-16}\mathrm{Scm}^{-1}\)) due to some kinetic limitations, as calculated by Cao et al.\(^{142}\) Kim et al. suggested that the composition of \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.55}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) in a voltage range of \(2.6 - 2.8\mathrm{V}\) is unfavorable because of electrostatic repulsion, which causes slow \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) diffusion.\(^{143}\) They suggested that Jahn-Teller distortion was another parameter of electrode performance decay. Recent work of Cao et al. reported the synthesis of single crystalline \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) nanowires with a reversible capacity that could be maintained for over 1000 cycles.\(^{142}\) High crystallinity provided the long-term durability for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction and the reduced diffusion path also contributed to excellent capacity retention.

The application of \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) electrodes has also been highlighted in aqueous systems. Whitcare et al. documented a full cell with a \(\mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) cathode and an activated carbon anode in a \(1\mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{NaSO}_4\) aqueous electrolyte.[144] Despite a smaller capacity in the aqueous solution relative to the aprotic ones, the full cell could be tested without apparent capacity loss over 1000 cycles. In the \(\mathrm{NaTi}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4) / \mathrm{Na}_{0.44}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) system, the cell was capable of operation at over 100C-rates and stable cycling for over 1000 cycles (Fig. 12b).[145]

\(\mathrm{Na}_{0.4}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) consists of a \(2 \times 3\) tunnel structure called romanechite.[89] \(\mathrm{Ba}^+\) and water molecules have been introduced to stabilize the large tunnel structure. Although the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction mechanism remains unclear, \(0.3\mathrm{mol}\) of the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion could be inserted into the host structure.

Few studies have explored Na-free \(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion. Because of the relatively large tunnel size of \(\alpha\)- and \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\), \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion is also possible for both \(\alpha\)- and \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\). \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\), which is called hollandite, is composed of double chains of edge-sharing \(\mathrm{MnO}_6\) octahedra that are linked at the corners to form a \(2\times 2\) and \(1\times 1\) tunnel structure (Fig. 12c). Because of the large size of the \(2\times 2\) tunnel, \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion into the empty tunnel is possible, as suggested by Su et al.\(^{146}\) and Islam et al.\(^{147}\) (Fig. 12d). A relatively large capacity of approximately \(280~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) was obtained at the first discharge in \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) nanorods, whereas the retained capacity was only \(75~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) after 100 cycles. In contrast, rutile-type \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) shows a 1D channel \(1\times 1\) tunnels along the \(c\)-axis composed of individual chains of the \(\mathrm{MnO}_6\) octahedral units. In general, insertion of ion species, i.e., \(\mathrm{Li}^+\), is not easy due to the small size of the \(1\times 1\) tunnels.\(^{148}\) Su et al. also tested \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) in Na cells, which delivers approximately \(300~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) during the first cycle.\(^{146}\) In comparison with \(\alpha\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\), the \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) nanorods exhibited a higher discharge capacity of approximately \(145~\mathrm{mA~h~g^{-1}}\) after 100 cycles. They attributed the better electrochemical performance of \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO}_2\) to the large number of empty tunnels that

accommodate the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions. Since both \(\alpha\)- and \(\beta\)-\(\mathrm{MnO_2}\) have a different tunnel structure, both materials should exhibit different charge and discharge behavior.\(^{149}\) Nevertheless, the same voltage profiles showing sloppy discharge curves are more likely to be related to pseudo-capacitance behavior. \(\lambda\)-Type \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\), which is produced by electrochemical delithiation of \(\mathrm{LiMn_2O_4}\), showed discharge of approximately \(200\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) during the first cycle with reasonable capacity retention.\(^{150}\) The \(\lambda\)-type \(\mathrm{MnO_2}\) phase was transformed into O\(^{\prime}3\) \(\mathrm{NaMnO_2}\) via electrochemical cycling and showed stable cycling performance.

2.2.2. Vanadium oxides. Vanadium oxides have been intensively studied as cathode materials for lithium-ion batteries. In Na cells, \(\alpha\)-, \(\beta\)-Na\(_x\)V\(_2\)O\(_5\), and \(\mathrm{Na}_{1+x}\mathrm{V}_3\mathrm{O}_8\) were investigated for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion insertion by West et al. in the 1980s. \(\alpha\)-V\(_2\)O\(_5\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_{1+x}\mathrm{V}_3\mathrm{O}_8\) have layered structures, and \(\beta\)-Na\(_x\)V\(_2\)O\(_5\) has a three-dimensional structure with wide channels.\(^{151}\) In experiments, upon \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion insertion, \(\alpha\)-V\(_2\)O\(_5\) underwent a phase transition resulting in a new structure. Meanwhile, sloppy charge and discharge curves were obtained for \(\beta\)-Na\(_x\)V\(_2\)O\(_5\). \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion insertion in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1-x}\mathrm{V}_3\mathrm{O}_8\) proceeded as a multi-phase reaction. Although structural evolution did not occur during \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion, the absence of a plateau on \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) extraction indicates the slow kinetics of Na extraction. Nevertheless, the capacity retention was above \(98\%\) for 100 cycles in a voltage range of 1-3.5 V.

Recently, Tapavcevic et al. reintroduced layered \(\alpha\text{-V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) using a nanostructured bilayer concept, of which the interslab distance was approximately \(13.5\AA\), which is significantly larger than that of conventional \(\mathrm{V}_2\mathrm{O}_5\) showing \(4.4\AA\). This was possible because they used electrochemical deposition from aqueous vanadyl sulfate on a Ni foil substrate followed by heat treatment at \(120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for removal of water molecules present in the interslabs (Fig. 13a). The electrode was activated by a \(\mathrm{V}^{5+/4+}\) redox reaction in a voltage range of \(1.5-3.8\mathrm{~V}\), but showed sloping charge-discharge curves. The electrode delivered a reversible capacity of approximately \(250\mathrm{~mA~h~g^{-1}}\), which is close to the theoretical capacity, with excellent cyclability of 300 cycles (Fig. 13b).

Surface modification of \(\mathrm{VO}_2(\mathrm{B})\) using reduced graphene oxide (rGO) substantially improved repetitive \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion ability.\(^{153}\) Although it has an open bronze structure to accommodate ion species into the empty channels, structural collapse or amorphization was observed in Na cells. The charge and discharge curves were sloppy, delivering approximately \(150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), in which the \(\mathrm{V}^{4 + / 3 + }\) reaction was related to the electrochemical reaction. Both \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion into \(\mathrm{VO}_2(\mathrm{B})\) and pseudo-capacitive behavior were confirmed via XRD and XAS studies.

2.2.3. Metal fluorides. Fluorine compounds have a high discharge voltage due to their ionic metal-ligand bonds. Perovskite-type metal trifluorides with a corner-sharing matrix \((R\bar{3}c)\) have large bottlenecks in diffusion pathways for ion carriers such as \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) and \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\). Although they have a high theoretical capacity (\(\sim 200\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)), their electrochemical performance is affected by the low electric conductivity of metal fluorides. Success has been achieved through mechanical milling and compositization with nanosized carbons. Okada's group introduced several metal fluorides (metal: Fe, V, Ti, Co, and Mn) including sodiated compounds.[154-157] A reversible

\(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+ / 2+}\) redox reaction achieved \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction, which could deliver \(150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) during the first cycle. All \(\mathrm{MF}_3\) compounds except \(\mathrm{FeF}_3\) (M: V, Ti, Co, and Mn) exhibited disappointing \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) storage. They further developed sodiated metal fluoride crystallized into the perovskite structure, \(\mathrm{NaMF}_3\) (M: Fe, Mn, Ni). Among those materials, \(\mathrm{NaFeF}_3\) had a relatively large discharge capacity (\(128\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)) with an average cell voltage of \(2.7\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 13c), while \(\mathrm{NaNiF}_3\) and \(\mathrm{NaMnF}_3\) suffered from capacities below \(40\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with sloppy voltage decay. Even when the synthesis method was switched from mechanomilling to a solution-based method, the resulting capacity did not exceed the prior report at the same current density (\(0.2\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{cm}^{-1}\)), but instead reached \(180\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at a rate of \(0.01\mathrm{C}\). The strong ionic character of the M-F bond must be overcome to achieve a high capacity even at high rates. Homogeneous dispersion of nanosized metal fluoride materials onto a conducting carbon matrix might facilitate high rate performance.
2.3. Three-dimensional polyanion compounds

In comparison with oxide and fluoride systems, transition metal polyanion materials have shown significant thermal stability, which is supported by the presence of covalent bonds such as P and O, in particular cathodes are in a deeply-charged

(oxidized) state. Oxygen evolution is common in layered compounds at temperatures above \(200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\);[51,85,158-162] however, such behavior is dramatically suppressed by the presence of P-O covalent bonds in the crystal structure. These phenomena prevail in Li cells and also apply to Na systems, since the related material chemistry during electrochemical reactions does not vary significantly from Li systems with the exception of the charge carrier, \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\), in Na cells. Polyanion-based materials usually exhibit lower electric conductivity relative to oxides, such that surface modifications using electro-conducting carbons, which contribute to a dramatic increase in electrical conductivity, improve electrochemical performance. The basic form begins from \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\); interestingly, some factors such as (i) variation in the charge carrier number of Na in Na sites, (ii) partial or full replacement of Fe by the other transition metals, (iii) a mixed anion system with F, OH, \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\), and (iv) extension towards mixed phosphate \((\mathrm{PO}_4)^{3-}\) and pyrophosphate \((\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)^{4-}\) ions also stabilize the crystal structure in the Na system. Unfortunately, because moisture absorption occurs easily in these polyanion systems, avoiding hydration and formation of NaOH on the surfaces of particles can be a challenge. Heterogeneous surfaces may lead to misinterpretation of the electrode performance in Na-containing aprotic electrolytes.

2.3.1. Phosphates and fluorophosphates. Maricite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) is a thermodynamically favored phase because it can be synthesized at high temperatures. Avdeev et al. demonstrated that chemically sodiated triphylite (olivine) \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) exhibited an irreversible phase transition from olivine to maricite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) at around \(480^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), resulting in a significant volume shrinkage.[163] Crystal structures of both polymorphs consist of slightly distorted \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\) octahedra and \(\mathrm{PO_4}\) tetrahedra. The maricite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) has edge-sharing \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\) units that share corners with neighboring \(\mathrm{PO_4}\). There are no cationic channels for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) movement (Fig. 14a).[164] In contrast, triphylite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) has corner-sharing \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\), which is linked with the \(\mathrm{PO_4}\) edge (Fig. 14b). A one-dimensional \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) diffusion channel is clearly seen along the \(b\)-axis in the crystal structure. The difference between these two polymorphs is the corner sharing and edge sharing \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\) chains for triphylite and maricite, respectively. However, direct synthesis of triphylite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) is not possible. Thus, chemical and electrochemical sodiation are effective ways to insert \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) into heterosite \(\mathrm{FePO_4}\) (space group, Pnma).

Electrode performance is dependent on the crystal structure. Amorphous \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) exhibited a high discharge capacity of \(\sim 150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), but operated at a low voltage (2.4 V) with a sloping discharge profile.[165] Maricite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) is electrochemically

inactive, as the structure blocks \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)extraction and insertion.\(^{166}\) Olivine \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) shows a relatively high operating voltage (2.8 V) with two distinct voltage plateaus, delivering over \(120\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) capacity (Fig. 14c).\(^{163-173}\) During the electrochemical reaction, the oxidation state of Fe is systematically altered followed by a redox reaction of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3 + / 2 + }\), as confirmed by the XAS studies performed by Ali et al.\(^{173}\) Carbon coating led to excellent long-term cyclability. An interesting feature of triphylite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) is the appearance of intermediate \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}FePO_4}\), which has the same crystal structure as the triphylite \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\), during electrochemical testing, whereas this phase is not found in \(\mathrm{LiFePO_4}\). Moreau et al. suggested that the advent of \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}FePO_4}\) could be related to cationic ordering in the crystal structure.\(^{164}\) Similar behavior is also observed in \(\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2^{62}\) in comparison with \(\mathrm{Li_xCoO_2}\).\(^{174}\) Casas-Cabanas et al. demonstrated that \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ion insertion into \(\mathrm{FePO_4}\) occurs via the intermediate phases due to large volume mismatches between \(\mathrm{FePO_4}\) and \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\).\(^{170}\) Therefore, three phases, \(\mathrm{FePO_4}\), \(\mathrm{Na_{0.7}FePO_4}\), and \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\), appear simultaneously on discharge, while they are separated into two first-order phase transitions on charge, which may be related to \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}/\)vacancy ordering in the crystal structure. In comparison with \(\mathrm{LiFePO_4}\), the charge transfer resistance of \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) is high, and the diffusion coefficient of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ions is

approximately 2 orders of magnitude lower.\(^{175}\) Recent density functional theory calculation revealed that the migration energy of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions in \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\) is \(0.05\mathrm{eV}\) higher than that of \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) ions in \(\mathrm{LiFePO_4}\), which reflects the slow kinetics in \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\).\(^{172}\) The large ion size of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) relative to \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) is associated with slow diffusion of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions into the crystal structure. An extension of \(\mathrm{NaFePO_4}\), \(\mathrm{Na[Fe_{0.5}Mn_{0.5}]PO_4}\), could be synthesized,\(^{168}\) however, the electrode did not show voltage plateaus, but rather a sloping curve due to the high interface strain between the Na-rich and Na-poor phases that was ascribed to the large size of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions, allowing \(0.6\mathrm{mol~Na^{+}}\) into \(\mathrm{Na}_x[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{PO}_4\). Since large \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are associated with migration, minimization of the interface energy is essential to the utilization of the other transition metals such as Co, Mn, and Ni, among others.

The ionicity of fluorides over oxides and sulfides is ascribed to their higher electronegativity, which thus increases the operating voltage of the electrodes. This concept was successfully applied to the discovery of \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FePO}_4\mathrm{F}\), which is applicable to both Li and Na systems.\(^{176}\) This material crystallizes in orthorhombic structures with a Pbcn space group (Fig. 14d). Bioctahedral \(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_7\mathrm{F}_2\) units comprising face-sharing \(\mathrm{FeO}_4\mathrm{F}_2\) octahedra are connected via bridging F atoms to form a chain, and are joined by \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) tetrahedra to form \(\mathrm{FePO}_4\mathrm{F}\) layers. The two Na cations located in the interlayer enable facile two-dimensional migration pathways. However, structural evolution from the layer structure to a tunnel one occurred (space group \(P2_{1}/n\)) when Fe was substituted for Mn greater than \(25\%\). In the case of the tunnel structure, the Na cations are located within channels.\(^{177}\) Notwithstanding the advantages of high operating voltage relative to \(\mathrm{NaFePO}_4\), the strong ionicity lowers the intrinsic electric conductivity. Hence, the bare \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FePO}_4\mathrm{F}\) delivered almost no capacity. Surprisingly, the carbon-coated \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FePO}_4\mathrm{F}\) could deliver approximately \(110\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), which corresponds to a \(90\%\) theoretical capacity.\(^{178}\) Two voltage plateaus are evident with small polarization. As anticipated from the crystal structure, Tripathi et al. predicted \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion conduction in \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FePO}_4\mathrm{F}\), which occurs along the two-dimensional interlayer with low activation energy.\(^{179}\) However, further investigation is needed to improve cycling performance by adding more carbon to provide better electron conduction. Carbon-coated \(\mathrm{Na}_2[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]\mathrm{PO}_4\mathrm{F}\) (\(6\mathrm{wt}\%\) of carbon) also showed a reversible capacity of \(110\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), confirming the feasibility of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion migration into/out of the tunnel structure.\(^{180}\) Polarization of the Mn redox is clearly greater than that of the \(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/3+}\) redox, which is similar to those observed in \(\mathrm{LiFePO}_4\) and \(\mathrm{LiMnPO}_4\).

In place of divalent transition metal elements, a trivalent element is also available in the form of \(\mathrm{NaVPO_4F}\). Barker et al. first introduced the material as an electrode material for hybrid-ion cells in 2003.[181] During the initial charging, \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are extracted from the host material while \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are plated on the surface of the Li metal anode in a Li cell. This process releases an atomically equivalent amount of lithium back into the electrolyte. Therefore, \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) ions are reinserted into the host material during the first discharge because Na is immobilized on the Li metal surface. \(\mathrm{NaVPO_4F}\) has tetragonal

symmetry with a \(I4/mmm\) space group, which is an isostructure of \(\alpha\text{-Na}_3\mathrm{Fe}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_2\mathrm{F}_3\). This structure demonstrates facile \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion diffusion through an extended three-dimensional framework constructed from \(\mathrm{VO}_4\mathrm{F}_2\) octahedra and \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) tetrahedra (Fig. 15a). \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions are present in the empty channel and diffuse along the \(c\)-axis. Coupling with a hard carbon anode, \(\mathrm{NaVPO_4F}\), led to two-step voltage responses (Fig. 15b), while the delivered discharge declined to less than \(50\%\) of the initial capacity (approximately \(82\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)). Zhao et al. found another crystal system in this compound: monoclinic \((C2/c)\) as the low temperature form and tetragonal \((I4/mmm)\) as the high temperature form. Cr-doping into the V site of \(\mathrm{NaVPO_4F}\) crystallized to the monoclinic phase showed a discharge capacity of \(80\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with \(91\%\) retention for 20 cycles. Sauvage et al. reinvestigated Na-V-P-O-F materials because the structural parallelism between \(\mathrm{NaVPO_4F}\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{Al}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_2\mathrm{F}\) phases was not straightforward owing to the difficulties encountered in determining the stoichiometry. They found that a \(10\mathrm{wt}\%\) excess of NaF could yield a single phase. Reliable refinement results were obtained assuming a crystal structure of \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_2\mathrm{F}\), which can be presented as \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VOPO}_4\mathrm{F}_{0.5}\) instead of \(\mathrm{NaVPO_4F}\). In this structure, \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions are disordered across two sites (8h and 8j) and the oxidation state of V is \(4+\) (Fig. 15c). The crystal structure is composed of a \(\mathrm{VO}_5\mathrm{F}\) octahedral and \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) tetrahedra sharing O vertices parallel to the \(ab\)-plane. Along the \(c\)-axis direction, \(\mathrm{VO}_5\mathrm{F}\) octahedra are connected via F vertices located in the same \(ab\)-plane as the disordered Na atoms. This structure could accommodate \(0.56\mathrm{Na}^+\) (approximately \(87\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)) per formula unit in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VOPO}_4\mathrm{F}_{0.5}\) with two different voltage plateaus, 3.6 and \(4\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 15d). In consideration of the theoretical value (\(156\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)) assuming a \(\mathrm{V}^{4+/5+}\) reaction, the delivered capacity can be improved further with electro-conducting coating layers. Due to structural and electrolytic instability, the capacity retention in the upper voltage region is not promising compared to the capacity delivered at the lower voltage plateau. These results, including capacity and retention, agree with an earlier report by Barker et al. The oxidation state of V was lowered to \(3.8+\) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VPO}_{4.8}\mathrm{F}_{0.7}\). In comparison with \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VOPO}_4\mathrm{F}_{0.5}\), the oxygen content was reduced slightly, but the equivalent content of F increased. Although structural refinement was performed based on space group \(P4_2/mmm\), the XRD pattern, including the Bragg peak position, is the same for both compositions. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VPO}_{4.8}\mathrm{F}_{0.7}\) is comprised of tetrahedral \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) and \(\mathrm{VO}_5\mathrm{F}/\mathrm{VO}_4\mathrm{F}_2\) octahedral units that share corners, which yields a three-dimensional open framework with \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions located at interstitial sites (Fig. 15e). \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VPO}_{4.8}\mathrm{F}_{0.7}\) provided a discharge capacity of approximately \(137\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) based on the \(\mathrm{V}^{3.8+/5+}\) redox reaction (\(1.2\mathrm{mol}\mathrm{Na}^+\) per formula unit in \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VPO}_{4.8}\mathrm{F}_{0.7}\)) with excellent cyclability for 500 cycles (Fig. 15f).

To further lower the average oxidation state of V efforts were made employing \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\mathrm{F}_3\) with space group \(P4_{2} / mnm\).<sup>188-190</sup> Very recently, Bianchini et al. revisited the \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\mathrm{F}_3\) compound to clarify its crystal structure, although the structure was successfully refined by Le Mine et al. in 1988.<sup>191</sup> They claimed that structural discrepancies in the literature originated from the possible existence of F and O mixing in the structure.<sup>182,188-190</sup>

A high-resolution diffraction study using synchrotron radiation revealed a suitable orthorhombic structure with space group Amam rather than a tetragonal structure with space group \(P4_{2}/mnm\) (Fig. 15g). Interestingly, when F was absent, the structure was crystallized to NASICON-type rhombohedral \(\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{V}_{2}(\mathrm{PO}_{4})_{3}\) with space group \(R\bar{3}c\), which will be addressed in Section 2.3.4. Similar to \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VOPO}_4\mathrm{F}_{0.5}\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.5}\mathrm{VPO}_{4.8}\mathrm{F}_{0.7}\), the presence of fluorine with phosphorus ions raises the operating voltage originating from the strong inductive effects of the anion group, even though the \(\mathrm{V}^{3+/4+}\) redox couple is available for delivery of high capacity (approximately \(120\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\)) in \(\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{V}_{2}(\mathrm{PO}_{4})_{3}\mathrm{F}_{3}\) compounds, with two voltage plateaus at 3.7 and 4.2 V (Fig. 15h). Computational calculations revealed that \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions located at the Na2 site are more readily extracted due to the lower diffusion barrier. Compared with two earlier reports by Barker et al.\(^{181}\) and Sauvage et al.\(^{186}\) this work is notable because variation in the anion configuration and the content of oxygen fluorine dramatically improved electrochemical performance, even at high rates.

2.3.2. Pyrophosphates. \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion was introduced in \((\mathrm{MoO}_2)_2\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) in 2003.\(^{192}\) The crystal structure was stabilized in an orthorhombic system with a Pnma space group, which is the same as olivine \(\mathrm{LiFePO_4}\). The presence of an empty channel enabled \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion into \((\mathrm{MoO}_2)_2\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\), with a discharge capacity of \(190\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) corresponding to \(3.1\mathrm{mol}\mathrm{Na}^+\) per formula unit in \((\mathrm{MoO}_2)_2\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\), although the rate capability was limited due to the large ionic size of the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion.

In 2008, Adam et al.193 introduced the crystal structure of \(\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{MnP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) and it was confirmed using \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) intercalation cathode materials.194,195 Recent developments in SIBs have motivated the exploration of facile \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) intercalation materials towards pyrophosphates. \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) with triclinic \(P\bar{1}\) was first studied by Honma et al.196 and Barpanda et al.,197 and the crystal structure was stabilized into the triclinic \(P\bar{1}\) phase (Fig. 16a). The structure was comprised of corner-sharing \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\) octahedra creating \(\mathrm{Fe}_2\mathrm{O}_{11}\) dimers, which are interconnected via cornersharing and edge-sharing with \(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) pyrophosphate groups. \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\) octahedra and \(\mathrm{PO_4}\) tetrahedra are connected in a staggered fashion, thus creating large tunnels along the [110] direction, which accommodate Na atoms at four distinct sites. However, these materials are not competitive cathode materials because of the two phosphate groups per transition metal in terms of capacity (theoretical capacity approaching \(97\mathrm{mAhg^{-1}}\) even though the related material chemistries are interesting. The appearance of stepwise voltage plateaus (roughly at 2.5 and \(3\mathrm{V})\) may indicate the presence of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}/\) vacancy ordering in the structure (Fig. 16b). An ex situ study suggested that a two-phase reaction prevails in the upper voltage plateau and a single phase reaction is dominant in the lower voltage region.198 According to calculation by Clark et al.,199 low activation energy was found for long-range diffusion in all crystallographic directions in \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) (M: Fe and Mn), indicating three-dimensional \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion diffusion. Therefore, electrochemical studies indicated moderate performance at high rates, which is related to the availability of three-dimensionality and low migration energy for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion diffusion in \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\). The good electrode performances of \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) were further evidenced
in an inorganic ionic liquid NaFSA-KFSA (FSA: bisfluorosulfonyl)amide) at \(363\mathrm{K}\).<sup>200</sup>

Ha et al. extended the composition to \(\mathrm{Na}_{3.32}\mathrm{Fe}_{2.34}(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)_2\) which can be represented as \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.66}\mathrm{Fe}_{1.17}\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\), of which the crystal structure is identical to \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\), but the new composition is able to increase the theoretical capacity to approximately \(110\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\). The first irreversible capacity was negligible and the lower voltage was shortened. The electrode performance was superior to that of \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) in terms of capacity retention and rate capability up to a rate of 10C. However, \(\mathrm{Na}_{3.32}[\mathrm{Fe}_{0.5}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.5}]_{2.34}(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)_2\) and \(\mathrm{Na}_{3.32}\mathrm{Mn}_{2.34}(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)_2\) had very low capacities, which was a controversial result of the computational study of Clark et al. The poor electrochemical activity seems to be similar to that of \(\mathrm{LiMnPO_4}\), which requires strategies for nanosizing and doping with the other divalent elements to improve electric conductivity.

Recent work of Barpanda et al. identified a new polymorph of \(\beta\text{-Na}_2\text{MnP}_2\text{O}_7\), which was also stabilized into a triclinic structure, \(P\bar{1}\). The structure consists of distorted \(\mathrm{MnO}_6\) octahedral and \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) tetrahedral blocks creating tunnels accommodating Na atoms along the [001] direction. The structure has corner-sharing \(\mathrm{MnO}_6\text{-MnO}_6\) (\(\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_{11}\)) dimers, which are in turn connected by \(\mathrm{PO}_4\text{-PO}_4\) (\(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\)) diphosphate units in a mixed-edge and corner-sharing fashion (Fig. 16c). A galvanostatic test of the electrode provides a reversible discharge capacity of \(80\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) associated with an average operating voltage of \(3.6\mathrm{V}\) (Fig. 16d). This coincided with earlier DFT calculations suggesting that this compound can undergo a \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+/3+}\) redox reaction at around \(3.65\mathrm{V}\). The sloppy discharge curve is the signature of \(\beta\text{-Na}_2\text{MnP}_2\text{O}_7\), which is different from \(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{FeP}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) operating with two distinct voltage plateaus. Structural investigation is further required to elucidate the reaction mechanism. They also suggest a new pyrophosphate oxyanionic framework compound, tetragonal \(\mathrm{Na}_2(\mathrm{VO})\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) with space group P4bm. This material belongs to the fresnoite family consisting of a \(\mathrm{VO}_5\) or \((\mathrm{VO})\mathrm{O}_4\) square pyramid and \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) tetrahedral units (Fig. 16e). Each \(\mathrm{VO}_5\) pyramid is connected to four independent PO tetrahedra in a corner-sharing fashion, thus forming \([\mathrm{VP}_2\mathrm{O}_{11}]\) units. Also, each \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) tetrahedron is connected to two \(\mathrm{VO}_5\) units and one \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) unit that share corners. The delivered capacity was approximately \(80\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\), which is close to the theoretical capacity (\(93\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\)) (Fig. 16f). The discharge curve was sloppy with the \(\mathrm{V}^{5+/4+}\) redox reaction showing an average operating voltage of \(3.8\mathrm{V}\). Apart from the above-mentioned compounds, the other pyrophosphates with \(\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{II})\), \(\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{II})\), \(\mathrm{Ti}(\mathrm{II})\), \(\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{II})\) and higher transition metal oxidation states have not yet been explored as host materials for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion.

2.3.3. Mixed phosphates. Mixed phosphates are composed of phosphate \((\mathrm{PO}_4)^{3-}\) and pyrophosphate \((\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)^{4-}\). This phase was recently discovered in a \(\mathrm{Na}_4\mathrm{M}_3(\mathrm{PO}_4)_2\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) (M: Mn, Co, Ni) system by Sanz et al.[204] These compounds crystallize in the orthorhombic structure with a \(Pn2_1a\) space group. The structure is built up from \(\mathrm{MO}_6\) octahedra and \(\mathrm{PO}_4\) groups with shared corners. \(\mathrm{M}_3\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_{13}\) blocks parallel to the bc plane are linked with the \(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\) unit along the \(a\)-axis (Fig. 17a). Four \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) sites are located in the three-dimensional channel, which can

In contrast to \(\mathrm{Na}_4\mathrm{Fe}_3(\mathrm{PO}_4)_2\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\), the appearance of a stepwise curve would be ascribed to the presence of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\)/vacancy ordering in the crystal structure. In an attempt to mitigate the multistep voltage plateaus, they synthesized \(\mathrm{Na}_4[\mathrm{Co}_{2.4}\mathrm{Mn}_{0.3}\mathrm{Ni}_{0.3}]\cdot\) \((\mathrm{PO}_4)_2\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7\). Another interesting compound, \(\mathrm{Na}_7\mathrm{V}_4(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)\mathrm{PO}_4\), was suggested by Lim et al. [208] This material has a crystallized tetragonal structure with a \(P\bar{4} 2_{1}c\) space group. The crystal structure has a basic unit, \((\mathrm{VP}_2\mathrm{O}_7)_4\mathrm{PO}_4\), which is composed of a central tetrahedron \([\mathrm{PO}_4]\) that shares corners with four \([\mathrm{VO}_6]\) octahedra, and each diphosphate group \([\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7]\) bridges the two adjacent \([\mathrm{VO}_6]\) octahedra with shared corners. The interconnected \((\mathrm{VP}_2\mathrm{O}_7)_4\mathrm{PO}_4\) units comprise a three-dimensional channel for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion diffusion (Fig. 17d). Based on the \(\mathrm{V}^{3 + / 4 +}\) redox reaction, \(\mathrm{Na}_7\mathrm{V}_4(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{O}_7)\mathrm{PO}_4\) exhibited a flat plateau at \(3.88\mathrm{~V}\) associated with a biphasic reaction governed by the first-order transition, delivering approximately \(90\mathrm{~mA~h~g^{-1}}\) (Fig. 17e). Addition of rGO further extended its cycle life to 1000 cycles with \(78\%\) capacity retention.

2.3.4. NASICON type phosphates. \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) , which crystallizes to a rhombohedral structure with a \(R\bar{3} c\) space group, has a NASICON-type framework built up from isolated \(\mathrm{VO}_6\) octahedra and \(\mathrm{PO_4}\) tetrahedral units interlinked via corners to form a framework anion, \([\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3]^{3 - }\) . Two different Na atoms are located in the empty channels of the framework with two different

oxygen environments (Fig. 18a).<sup>209</sup> Early work by Gopalakrishnan et al. demonstrated the possibility of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)extraction to form \(\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) using chlorine in \(\mathrm{CHCl}_2\) at room temperature.<sup>210</sup> Electrochemical \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)extraction was investigated by Masquelier<sup>211</sup> and Plashnitsa et al.<sup>212</sup> and two voltage plateaus were found at 3.4 and \(1.6\mathrm{~V}\). The latter work also tested a full symmetric cell, adopting \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) for both cathodes, but the performance was unsatisfactory. Jian et al. modified the surface of \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) using electro-conducting carbons derived from sugar.<sup>209</sup> The \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3/\mathrm{C}\) electrode exhibited improved capacity in both the upper and lower voltage regions compared with earlier works (Fig. 18b). They further reduced the irreversible capacity, changing the conventional electrolyte to NaFSI/PC from the PC-based compound. Jian et al. further analyzed the crystal structure during the electrochemical reaction.[215] As mentioned in Fig. 18a, Na atoms are localized to two different sites (6b, M1 and 18e, M2) in \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\). The flat curve during desodiation of \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) indicates a biphasic reaction based on the \(\mathrm{V}^{3+/4+}\) redox reaction as a result of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) extraction at the M2 site, which is related to the formation of \(\mathrm{NaV}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)\), in which only one Na (6b, M1) resides in the crystal structure. NMR further revealed that, in \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\), the Na atoms were not randomly distributed at M2 sites, but appeared in an ordered arrangement locally; an \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion at the M2 sites in \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) is mobile, whereas an \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion at the M1 site is immobile, corresponding to \(2\mathrm{mol}\mathrm{Na}^+\) per formula unit in \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\), while \(1\mathrm{mol}\mathrm{Na}^+\) per formula unit in \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{PO}_4)_3\) can retain the framework during the electrochemical reaction.

2.3.5. Sulfates, fluorosulfates, and carbonophosphates. Substitution of \((\mathrm{SO}_4)^{2-}\) for the \((\mathrm{PO}_4)^{3-}\) anion results in a higher operating voltage because of the greater ionicity. When successful, this improves the operating voltage in a favorite-type material, leading to an OCV increase of \(0.6 - 0.8\mathrm{~V~}\) in \(\mathrm{LiFeSO_4F}\) compared to \(\mathrm{LiFePO_4F}\). Similarly, this substitution is also applicable in Na systems, namely, \(\mathrm{NaMSO_4F}\) (M: Fe, Co, and Mn), as suggested by Barpanda et al. The compounds are crystallized in a monoclinic structure with a \(P2_{1}/c\) space group (Fig. 19a). Tripathi et al. calculated the activation energy for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) diffusion. Although \(\mathrm{NaFeSO_4F}\) has a three-dimensional structure in only one direction, [010], it has relatively low activation energy \((0.6\mathrm{eV})\). The measured ionic conductivity of the compound was sufficiently high at approximately \(10^{-7}\mathrm{Scm}^{-1}\). Notwithstanding, the electrode delivered only \(6\%\) of the theoretical capacity \((137\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) (Fig. 19b). Barpanda et al. further developed a sulfate-based sodiated iron compound, which was crystallized to an alluaudite-type sulfate framework, \(\mathrm{Na_2Fe_2(SO_4)_3}\), with a \(P2_{1}/c\) space group. In this case, the crystal structure was similar to earlier work on \(\mathrm{NaMSO_4F}\), while the resulting volume of the unit cell was larger by over two-fold. In contrast to \(\mathrm{A_xM_2(XO_4)_3}\)-type compounds, which usually have NASICON-related structures, \(\mathrm{Na_2Fe_2(SO_4)_3}\) does not contain \([\mathrm{M}_2(\mathrm{XO}_4)_3]\) units, but has a unique structure with an alluaudite type framework (Fig. 19c). \(\mathrm{FeO_6}\) octahedra share edges, forming \(\mathrm{Fe_2O_{10}}\) dimer units. These Fe ions could be assigned to two distinct groups, \(\mathrm{Fe(1)}\) and \(\mathrm{Fe(2)}\), since local structures of \(\mathrm{Fe(1)}\) and \(\mathrm{Fe(2)}\) were found to have two doublets in the Mössbauer spectra. The \(\mathrm{Fe_2O_{10}}\) dimers were linked with \(\mathrm{SO}_4\) units in a corner-sharing fashion, generating a three-dimensional network framework with large tunnels along the c-axis, with Na located in the tunnels. Electrochemical testing of the material revealed that a \(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/3+}\) redox reaction led to a high operating voltage of \(3.8\mathrm{~V~}\) on average, the highest that appeared in the \(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/3+}\) reaction, which confirms strong ionicity when \((\mathrm{SO}_4)^{2-}\) anions occur in the crystal structure instead of \((\mathrm{PO}_4)^{3-}\). Based on one-electron transfer, the material could deliver approximately \(85\%\) of the theoretical capacity \((120\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) with a moderate rate capability (Fig. 19d). The structure may be able to extend to several family compounds, adopting not only Fe,
but the other transition metal elements such as Ni, Co, V, and Mn, among others.

Sociated metal carbonophosphates, such as sidorenkite \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{MPO}_4\mathrm{CO}_3\) (M: Mg, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu), were recently introduced as a new intercalation cathode by Chen et al. [220,221] Two different series of \((\mathrm{PO}_4)_3^-\) and \((\mathrm{CO}_2)^2\) -ions have bonds with M in \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{MPO}_4\mathrm{CO}_3\) compounds, which are stable with a sidorenkite structure and a \(P2_{1} / m\) space group (Fig. 19e). In \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{MnPO}_4\mathrm{CO}_3\), the \(\mathrm{MnO_6}\) octahedra are connected by a \(\mathrm{PO_4}\) tetrahedra to form a double layer. The \(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\) group shares an oxygen edge with \(\mathrm{MnO_6}\). The two-dimensional interslab accommodates two Na atoms at two different interstitial sites, where Na1 and Na2 coordinate with seven and six O atoms, respectively. As anticipated, their computations suggested that the sidorenkite structure may have good intercalation properties. [222] Carbophosphate showed high discharge \((125\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) (Fig. 19f), which represented \(66\%\) of the theoretical capacity \((191\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1})\). It is interesting that \(\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{MPO}_4\mathrm{CO}_3\) underwent a topotactic solid solution reaction during cycling, activated by a two-electron electrochemical reaction of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2 + / 3 + }\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + / 4 + }\) redox couples.

2.3.6. Cyanides and organic compounds. Prussian blue and its analogues were investigated as hosts for alkali ions, but those works received less attention due to low energy density compared with the above-mentioned materials.[223,224] Recently, Cui and coworkers reported potassium copper hexacyanoferrate to intercalate \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions in aqueous solution at less than \(2\mathrm{V}\).[225-227] In an attempt to increase the operation voltage, Wang et al. utilized \(\mathrm{KMFe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\) (M: Fe and Mn, which are divalent with high spin), which exhibited high operation voltage plateaus at \(3.8\mathrm{V}\) for charge and \(3.5\mathrm{V}\) for discharge.[228] Their extended work varied the concentration of Na in the \(\mathrm{MFe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\) perovskite framework: \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.4}\mathrm{MFe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\) (\(Fm3m\)) and \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.72}\mathrm{MFe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\) (\(R\bar{3}m\))[229]. The double-perovskite ordering of both the anion orientations and the cations introduces a rhombohedral site symmetry along each of the four [111] axes that would stabilize alkali-ion displacement along a cubic [111] axis toward the more octahedral-site complex. The four-fold degeneracy of these displacements keeps the cubic phase at higher temperature and lower Na concentration, but a cooperative Na displacement at higher Na concentration reduces the crystal symmetry to rhombohedral, stabilizing the rhombohedral phase (Fig. 20a). Note that the larger is the concentration of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions in the compound, the higher is the temperature of transition from rhombohedral to cubic symmetry. Electrochemical reactions occurring at low and high voltage are the redox reactions of low-spin \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+/2+}\) and high-spin \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+/2+}\), respectively. The post-cycled electrode did not show a notable structural change after 50 cycles. \(\mathrm{Na}_{1.72}\mathrm{MFe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\) reacted at a rate of 40C (Fig. 20b). Replacement of Mn with Fe, \(\mathrm{FeFe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\), lowered the operation voltage of the lower voltage plateau to \(3.1\mathrm{V}\) on charge and \(2.8\mathrm{V}\) on discharge, delivering a discharge capacity of approximately \(120\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\).[230] The electrode was substantially stable over 600 cycles with excellent capacity retention. \(\mathrm{Na}_4\mathrm{Fe}[\mathrm{CN}]_6\) was electrochemically active in Na cells, showing approximately \(90\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) on discharge with a flat voltage plateau at \(3.4\mathrm{V}\).[231] The lower capacity is due to the one-electron reaction by the low-spin \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+/2+}\) redox.

3. Anode materials

As mentioned in Section 2, several platforms of cathode materials have been introduced for SIBs.[239] Simultaneously, as the cathode counterpart, the recent development of anodes for SIBs has been achieved by the use of selected carbonaceous materials, transition metal oxides (or sulfides) and intermetallic and

organic compounds, as anodes.\(^{240}\) Research on the above anode materials for SIBs has been developed in three main categories, based on the reaction mechanism during sodiation/ desodiation processes: (1) the insertion reaction, (2) the conversion reaction, and (3) the alloying reaction. Carbonaceous materials and titanium-based oxides have been introduced as insertion anode materials.\(^{241,242}\) Recent efforts expand the applications towards transition metal oxides (TMOs) or transition metal sulfides (TMSs) that are driven by a conversion reaction.\(^{243}\) Also, Na-Me \((\mathrm{Me} = \mathrm{metal})\) alloying compounds of group 14 or 15 elements motivated by the alloying reaction are recommended as possible candidate materials with the help of an electro-conducting carbon matrix to improve their specific capacity and cyclability.\(^{244,245}\) Such conversion and alloying reaction materials are known to deliver high capacities but suffer from huge volume expansion of the host materials due to

the continuous self-pulverization of the electrode materials.\(^{240,243}\) More seriously, to compete with lithium ion batteries, a great challenge to overcome is the sluggish reaction kinetics from the large ionic size of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions (1.02 Å), which impedes fast \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) storage.\(^{99,240,246}\) Therefore, scientists should make more efforts to improve the electrode performances. In this section, we discuss present research progress in anode materials for SIBs.
3.1. Insertion materials

Based on an insertion reaction, carbonaceous and titanium-based oxides have been extensively studied as anodes for SIBs. Several carbon-based materials such as graphitic and non-graphitic carbons have been investigated for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) storage.[241] These carbon materials are widely accepted because of their ability to accommodate \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions into their structure. In particular, hard carbon is interesting because of its reasonable capacity of \(\sim 300\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) and low operating potential (almost zero, \(\sim 0\mathrm{V}\) vs. \(\mathrm{Na}^+/ \mathrm{Na}\)).[22,23,247] However, the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) storage mechanism in a disordered carbon structure is still controversial.[247-251] On the other hand, titanium-based oxide compounds have been widely studied because of their low operation voltage and cost as well.[242] Analogous to LIBs, titanium-based oxide anodes, including various polymorphs titanium dioxide \((\mathrm{TiO}_2)\), spinel-lithium titanate \((\mathrm{Li}_4\mathrm{Ti}_5\mathrm{O}_{12})\) and sodium titanate \((\mathrm{Na}_x\mathrm{Ti}_y\mathrm{O}_z)\), were reported as promising anode materials.
3.1.1. Carbon-based anode materials

3.1.1.1. Graphitic carbon (graphite). During the electrochemical reduction, \(\mathrm{Li^{+}}\) ions are inserted between graphene layers, and Li-graphite intercalation compounds (Li-GIC) are formed through stage transformations in Li cells.[252,253] Since the 1980s, the electrochemical behavior of graphite with \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions has been studied based on the graphite/polyethylene oxide \(\mathrm{NaCF_3SO_3 / Na}\) cell[254] (Fig. 21a). However, \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion into graphite is significantly impeded and degradation of electrolyte and/or electrode materials was observed[255] (Fig. 21b). First-principles calculation results of the formation energy for Na-GIC exhibited that Na hardly intercalates into graphite because of the energetic instability of the Na-GICs. Graphite is stressed when some \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) intercalates into graphite because of the thermodynamic instability of binary Na-intercalated GICs (b-GIC), which is assumed to be the result of an unfavorable mismatch between the graphite structure and the size of the Na ion.[256] Namely, due to the unfavorable formation of \(\mathrm{NaC_6}\) and \(\mathrm{NaC_8}\) that are thermodynamically unstable at the first stage of Na-graphite intercalation compound (Na-GIC) formation in SIBs, the capacity of natural graphite is limited.[256-258] As a recent breakthrough, Jache et al. facilitated Na-ion storage in graphite using solvated-Na-ion intercalation, forming ternary GICs. That is the intercalation of solvated alkali ions "co-intercalation" by reduction of graphite according to the following equation: \(\mathrm{C_n + e^- + A^+ + y\mathrm{sol}v\leftrightarrow A^+(solv)_yC_n^{-}}\) [258] Zhu et al. also confirmed that solvated \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions intercalate into graphite via a stageevolution process, forming a set of ternary graphite intercalation compounds.[259] Ether-based electrolytes with a high donor number can form stable \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) solvated species with non-polar characteristics for co-intercalation into natural graphite.[260-262]

Kim et al. reported a reversible phase transition during intercalation and deintercalation processes of solvated-Na-ions in natural graphite via an operando X-ray diffraction analysis\(^{261}\) (Fig. 21c). They investigated the unusual Na storage behavior in natural graphite through \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)-solvent co-intercalation using different solvents (EC/DEC, DME, DEGDME and TEGDME) and Na salts (\(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\), \(\mathrm{NaClO_4}\), \(\mathrm{NaCF_3SO_3}\)). Ether-based electrolytes could suppress electrolyte decomposition, resulting in the formation of a negligible SEI film on the graphite surface, enabling \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)-solvent transport to the graphite lattice. In contrast, carbonate-based electrolytes (EC/DEC) form relatively thick insulating SEI layers on the graphite surface, which block \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)-solvent transport\(^{262}\) (Fig. 21d). As a result, under the limited conditions using ether-based electrolytes, the natural graphite delivered a capacity of \(150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with reasonable retention for 2500 cycles, and produced over \(75\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at \(10\mathrm{A}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) in the DEGDME electrolyte containing \(\mathrm{NaPF_6}\) salt. On the other hand, Wen et al. proposed expanded graphite with an enlarged interlayer lattice distance of \(4.3\AA\). Note that graphite has a typical interlayer space of \(3.4\AA\).\(^{263}\) Their in situ TEM study revealed reversible insertion and extraction of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions during the electrochemical reaction (Fig. 21e). As a result, the expanded graphite could exhibit a moderate capacity of approximately \(284\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at a current of \(20\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with good capacity retention over 2000 cycles (Fig. 21f). Recently, Kang et al. reported the sodium ion intercalation behavior of expanded graphite oxide (GO) as an anode material. According to their report, the electrochemical properties of GO strongly depend on the amounts and ratios of different functional groups.\(^{264}\)

3.1.1.2. Non graphitic carbon (hard carbon). In 1993, Doeff and co-workers first reported the Na storage performance of disordered soft carbon prepared by pyrolysis of petroleum coke[251] (Fig. 22a). They demonstrated the extent of the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion/extraction reaction into the soft carbon and discussed the possibility of its application for SIBs. Stevens and Dahn reported the insertion mechanism of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions into disordered hard carbon (Fig. 22b).[22,23,247] The suggested mechanism was the "house of cards" for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion storage, which is composed of the two domains in a disordered hard carbon structure without staging transition. First, \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are inserted between parallel graphene sheets (in the sloping voltage region) upon increasing the interlayer space. Second, \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions fill the nanopores (in the plateau region) of the disordered carbon structure.[22,23,249] The hard carbon prepared by carbonization of glucose showed a reversible electrochemical reaction in Na cells and it delivered a high specific capacity of approximately \(300\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with a low operating potential of about \(\sim 0\mathrm{V}\). Later, Komaba and co-workers performed a systematic study on electrochemical sodium insertion into hard carbon to understand the related structural change.[27] Upon reduction to \(0.2\mathrm{V}\) (in the sloping region), the resulting XRD peaks shifted to lower angles, indicating that the interlayer spacing between the graphene sheets was expanded due to the sodium insertion (Fig. 22c). They also confirmed the reversible \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion process into the nanopores via small angle X-ray scattering measurements

-16 ppm was ascribed to the appearance of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ions in heterogeneously distributed closed nanopores in hard carbon.[265] In 2011, Wenzel and co-workers demonstrated high capacity carbon anode materials with an excellent rate capability of \(100\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at C/5 at room temperature for the first time[266] (Fig. 22f). co-relationships between the physico-chemical properties and the electrochemical properties with commercially available porous and non-porous carbon materials (porous carbon: Timrex 100, 300, 500 and activated carbon, non-porous carbon: graphite). The highest surface area \((1041\mathrm{m}^2\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) of activated carbon and the highest pore volume \((1.008\mathrm{cm}^3\mathrm{g}^{-1})\) of Timrex 500 show the low reversibility with \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions. Meanwhile, their templated carbon controlled by microstructural approaches minimized the diffusion lengths within the electrode (surface area: \(346\mathrm{m}^2\mathrm{g}^{-1}\), pore volume: \(0.798\mathrm{cm}^3\mathrm{g}^{-1}\)). As a result, the carbon matrix showed excellent electrochemical activity for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions. Their results imply that high capacity and rate capabilities cannot be simply achieved by enlarging the surface area or by increasing the porosity. On the other hand, Tsai et al. further confirmed the reaction process through an ab initio study showing that a larger interlayer distance is not the only factor that helps Na intercalation. Additionally, the vacancy defects (MV: mono-vacancy and DV: di-vacancy) in hard carbon can greatly enhance the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion intercalation because of the strong ionic binding energy between the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions and the defects, which effectively overcomes the van der Waals interaction.[248] Further, Bommier et al. discussed the new storage mechanism in the sloping region (as a function of the charge-discharge curve), which can be explained through \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion storage at defect sites.[250] They also confirmed that \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions can be intercalated into the hard carbon lattice particularly in the low voltage plateau region. These findings suggest that the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) storage is related to a three-step process (Fig. 22d) rather than the two-step storage mechanism proposed in the card-house model (Fig. 22e); (i) \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are adsorbed at defective sites in the slope-voltage region, (ii) \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are intercalated in the hard carbon lattice, and (iii) \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions are adsorbed at the pore surface in the plateau region. Such research results show that the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion mechanism into a disordered structure is still controversial. Therefore, further theoretical and experimental investigation should be conducted to clarify the related reaction mechanism for development of hard carbon materials.

To date, hard carbons have been the most widely used carbon source of SIB anodes and have been proved to significantly improve the electrochemical performance of SIBs. However, there are a lot of challenges facing the development of hard carbon anodes for practical applications. The reversibility of the hard carbon was found to depend on the carbon precursor, particle sizes and manufacturing processes. And, the appropriate low pore volumes and surface areas can achieve the higher reversible capacities. The suitable additives and electrolytes are highly desirable for reversibility of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) storage. Therefore, based on a number of previous studies, we should consider some factors, including particle sizes, additives, electrolytes, vacancy defects and porosity measurements, to develop the high irreversible capacity and high rate capability in hard carbons. Furthermore, we should investigate the relationship between the electrochemical characteristics of hard carbon and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer formation. At the same time, the computational works such as DFT calculation and ab initio calculation should be conducted to predict and support the experimental results.

3.1.1.3. Non graphitic carbon (graphene). Graphene, unique two-dimensional carbon materials, has great physical and chemical advantages such as a large surface area, superior electronic conductivity, and chemical stability.[267] Such advantages of graphene can bring shorter paths for fast ion diffusion and produce a large exposed surface offering more ion insertion channels.[268] Therefore, for LIBs and SIBs, graphene can be considered as an anode material for energy-storage systems[255,269-274] (Fig. 21b). Moreover, graphene or reduced graphene oxide (RGO) help to improving the Li and Na storage capability in its composite anodes. Recently, Wang et al. demonstrated the reversible sodium ion storage performances in reduced graphene oxide (RGO).[274] RGO possesses higher electrical conductivity and active sites with large interlayer distances and provides a disordered structure enabling it to store a larger amount of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions. In their study, RGO anodes exhibited a moderate specific capacity of \(141\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at \(40\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with stable cycle retentions for over 1000 cycles. Ding et al. synthesised different kinds of few-layer graphene at different carbonization temperatures \((600 - 1400^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\) and investigated the \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion storage mechanism in their graphene structure.[275] At a higher carbonization temperature of \(1100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), they obtained an optimized highly ordered pseudographic structure having a large interlayer spacing of \(0.388\mathrm{nm}\), which exhibited promising \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion performances. On the other hand, Datta et al. reported that the presence of defects enhances the adsorption of Na atoms in graphene sheets.[276] However, such defects of graphene represent a serious drawback such as low Coulombic efficiency that may cause Na metal plating on the rGO surfaces.[255]

3.1.1.4. Heteroatom doping. As an effective strategy to enhance the electrochemical properties of carbonaceous materials as anodes, heteroatom (such as N, B, S and P) doped hard carbon and graphene (or graphene liked materials) are introduced.[277-291] Hetero atom doping into a carbon structure tends to create a defect site to absorb \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions and improves the electrode-electrolyte interaction by functionalizing the carbon surface. Commonly, nitrogen doping into a carbon structure significantly improves ion transport and charge-transfer processes.[279,280] Wang et al. proposed a 3D interconnected structure of freestanding flexible films composed of nitrogen-doped porous nanofibers that exhibited \(212\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at \(5\mathrm{A}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with stable capacity retentions of \(99\%\) after 7000 cycles[282] (Fig. 23a). In a recent work, sulfur-doped disordered carbon was proposed as an anode material for SIBs. Sulfur doping into the carbon structure provided additional reaction sites for accommodation of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) and/or contributing to facile ion diffusion by enlarging the interlayer distance. Li et al. synthesized a sulfur-doped disordered carbon that has a high sulfur doping level (\(\sim 26.9\%\)) and a unique 3D coral-like structure. The as-prepared sulfur-doped carbon exhibited a high reversible capacity of \(516\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with excellent rate capability as well as superior cyclability for 1000 cycles[288] (Fig. 23b).
3.1.1.5. Biomass derivatives. As mentioned above, disordered carbon appears to be the most suitable anode material for SIBs.

Biomass-derived disordered carbon materials have been widely investigated due to their low production cost and low energy consumption during the synthesis procedures.[275,292-297] Wood carbon anodes were derived from natural woods via a simple carbonization process, which functions as a binder-free and current collector-free anode material.[292] This material exhibited a high areal capacity of \(13.6\mathrm{mA}\) h cm\(^{-2}\) with a high mass loading of \(55\mathrm{mgcm}^{-2}\). A recent work, reported by Yang et al., showed a sustainable route using a biomass byproduct, okara, as a natural nitrogen fertilizer possessing high content N-doped carbon sheets.[296] Okara was carbonized and exfoliated to expand between carbon sheets that led to a high specific surface area. This benefits facile intercalation and deintercalation of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ions into the carbon structure upon cycling. This unique structure and large surface area were responsible for the high capacity of approximately \(292.2\mathrm{mA}\) h g\(^{-1}\) with stable cycling performances for over 2000 cycles (Fig. 23c). From the green and recycle points
of view, using biomass (bio-waste) is of great significance for the future direction of material development.

Such works present that carbonaceous materials exhibit promising \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ion storage performances as anodes for SIBs. Notwithstanding, the initial irreversible capacity, limited capacity, poor rate capability, and low mass loading should be resolved for cell balancing for practical battery applications. Herein, we have mentioned few relevant ones and suggest the possible propositions as follows. The irreversible capacity upon the initial cycle is the most critical issue as it severely hindered the practical cell energy density achievable. Attaining an attractive rate capability is another important factor for a practical energy storage system. In addition, tap density is also a crucial parameter determining the volumetric energy density of the cells. The appropriate low pore volumes and surface areas were required for higher reversible capacities. Using suitable electrolytes, additives, and binders is also an effective approach

And morphology and size control is a substantial strategy to facilitate mass transport and storage, which can significantly improve the rate capability. Most of all, for developing practical SIBs using high mass loading electrodes, a comprehensive study of the impact of the electrode thickness on the rate capability, energy and power density and long-term cycling behavior is required.[298]

3.1.2 Titanium based oxides. In general, the low operational potential can cause safety issues for practical applications; such as, metallic sodium plating and sodium dendrite formation on the surfaces of anodes.[241,247,249,250,347] Analogous to LIBs, metal oxide compounds have been studied as \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ion insertion host materials. Titanium-based oxides are particularly interesting as anodes due to their reasonable operation voltage, cost, and nontoxicity,[299,300] representatively, titanium dioxides,[301-333] spinel-lithium titanate,[334-346] and sodium-titanate compounds.[347-364] Note that these compounds are driven by a \(\mathrm{Ti}^{4+/3+}\) redox couple in Na cells. Recently, most related works have focused on finding the sodiation/desodiation mechanism and improving the electrochemical performance of such materials.

3.1.2.1. Titanium dioxides. The several \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) polymorphs, including anatase- \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) \(^{301 - 314,316 - 323}\) rutile- \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) \(^{324 - 328}\) brookite\(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) \(^{329,330,332}\) and bronze- \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) \(^{333}\) have been investigated as anode materials for SIBs \(^{294 - 296}\) (Fig. 24a-d). Among them, most research results were reported using anatase \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) because the activation barrier for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion into the anatase lattice is comparable to that of lithium, which is rather remarkable considering its significantly larger ionic radius. \(^{301 - 303}\) Xiong et al. reported the feasibility of electrochemically-grown amorphous titanium dioxide nanotube electrodes in Na cells for the first

time\(^{304}\) (Fig. 25a). On the other hand, Mattsson et al. claimed that high crystalline and/or micronized \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) cannot easily support \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion because of the ionic size of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) and a much higher sodium diffusion barrier compared with Li.\(^{305}\) Recently, high electrochemical activity of \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) with \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions was achieved by reducing the particles to the nanometer size for shortening of the migration length for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion. Xu et al. used anatase nanocrystalline \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) for \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) storage.\(^{306}\) Wu et al. interpreted the reaction process during \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction, highlighting the substantial influence of the electrolyte composition (salt and solvent) and cut-off potential\(^{307}\) (Fig. 25b). Through NMR and electrochemical studies, Gonzalez et al. explained the irreversible process (electrolyte decomposition) up to \(0.3\mathrm{V}\) observed at the first \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion process in the \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) electrode.\(^{308}\) Kim et al. observed that anatase \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) nanorods can be stored in the host structure through the \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction reaction coupled with the \(\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}/\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}\) redox reaction via ex situ XRD and XAS studies\(^{309}\) (Fig. 25c and d). Their carbon coating on the anatase \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) nanorod surface played an important role in improving the capacity and rate capability. Furthermore, they also suggested that the presodiation technique was an effective way to minimize the initial irreversible reaction of the \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) anode. Recently, Passerini's group found that only the (de-)insertion of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) ions in the newly formed amorphous sodium titanate phase appears to be reversible (uptake/release 0.41 Na per \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\)), while all the other processes (metallic titanium, sodium superoxide, and oxygen evolution) appear to be irreversible (Fig. 25e and f).\(^{310}\) Usui et al. investigated rutile-\(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) that showed the reversible reaction of \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) insertion and extraction into and from the crystal lattice of rutile \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\)\(^{326}\) (Fig. 26a). In addition, the appropriate amount of Nb doping (0.06 mol%) on rutile \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) materials could substantially improve the electronic conductivity.

As a result, the \(\mathrm{Ti}_{0.94}\mathrm{Nb}_{0.06}\mathrm{O}_2\) electrode exhibited excellent cycling performance with a reversible capacity of \(160\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at the 50th cycle. Later, Zhang et al. proposed rutile \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) microspheres anchored by nanoneedle clusters, which exhibited a capacity retention of \(83.1\%\) after 200 cycles at a current density of 0.1C-rate.[327] Huang et al. found that one of the polymorphs, the low crystalline brookite-\(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) was available for \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\)ion storage.[329] Pioneer works of unusual \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) polymorphs, including the brookite-phase, the bronze-phase and their mixture phase, are recently investigated.[330-333]

However, sodium storage of \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) suffers from the sluggish sodium kinetics due to the larger ionic size of \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) ions. To overcome this drawback, strategies such as the nanoarchitecture modified by metallic Ti and high conductivity carbon additives were introduced.[309,311,328,331] Kim et al. fabricated pitch carbon-coated anatase \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) nanorods, which exhibited a high capacity of \(193\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) and superior rate capability.[309] Recently, Zhang et al. proposed graphene-rich wrapped petal-like rutile \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) tuned by carbon dots, which exhibited the outstanding sodium-storage performances.[328]

Chen et al. proposed a chemically bonded graphene (G)-\(\mathrm{TiO}_2(\mathrm{B})\) composite as a long-cycle life material. Kinetics analysis reveals \(\mathrm{Na}^+\) intercalation pseudo-capacitive behaviour in the G-\(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) sodium cell, which is highly beneficial to fast charge storage and long-term cyclability[331] (Fig. 26b).

Doping to reduce the average oxidation state of Ti is another interesting strategy to enhance the reversible Na storage performances of \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\).<sup>312,313,315,323</sup> Hwang et al. reported ultrafast sodium storage performance in fluorine-doped anatase \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) nanoparticles embedded on carbon nanotubes<sup>312</sup> (Fig. 26c). Nanosized anatase \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) partially doped with fluorine \((\mathrm{TiO_{2 - \delta}F_{\delta}})\) to form electro-conducting trivalent \(\mathrm{Ti^{3 + }}\) led to facile \(\mathrm{Na^{+}}\) insertion into an anatase \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) structure. In addition, \(\mathrm{TiO_{2 - \delta}F_{\delta}}\) was modified by electro-conducting carbon nanotubes (CNTs) to further enhance the electric conductivity. Boron doping can also enhance the photocatalytic activity of \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) due to or partly due to the formation of \(\mathrm{Ti^{3 + }}\) ions induced by oxygen vacancies which can increase the conductivity of \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\).<sup>315</sup> Recently, Wang et al. prepared B-doped \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) via the facile hydrothermal method and

demonstrated a high reversible capacity of \(150\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at a high current rate of 2C with stable cycling performance for over 400 cycles.\(^{313}\) Ni et al. introduced self-supported S-doped \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) nanotubes with high electronic conductivity\(^{323}\) (Fig. 26d). When S is incorporated into \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\), the S 3p states will be partially delocalized. The S 3p states can contribute to the formation of the valence band, and thus increases the width of the valence band, resulting in a decreased band gap energy (\(\mathrm{S - TiO_2}\): 2.6 eV, \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\): 3.0 eV).\(^{334}\) The high conductivity assisted in decreasing the band gap energy, which enabled delivery of a high capacity of \(320\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{h}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) at \(33.5\mathrm{mA}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\) with a stable capacity retention of \(91\%\) for over 4400 cycles at a high current density of \(3.35\mathrm{A}\mathrm{g}^{-1}\).

3.1.2.2. Lithium titanate. A spinel \(\mathrm{Li}_4\mathrm{Ti}_5\mathrm{O}_{12}\) has been extensively studied as one of the most promising anode materials for long-life stationary LIBs because it has a flat and high potential around \(1.5\mathrm{V}\) (vs. \(\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}\)) during charge and discharge and an excellent cycle life due to the negligible volume change.[335] Recently, Zhao et al. revisited spinel \(\mathrm{Li}_4\mathrm{Ti}_5\mathrm{O}_{12}\) as a \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)insertion host material for SIBs[300,336]
